This is the fourth time he has been caught. His mother mentions he also struggles with frequent episodes of binge eating. Which condition is most strongly associated with this patient's behavior?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize the strong association between kleptomania and bulimia nervosa.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has kleptomania (stealing unneeded low-value items for a rush/relief). Kleptomania has a high comorbidity rate (65%) with Bulimia Nervosa.

metadata.reviewPearl: Recognize the strong association between kleptomania and bulimia nervosa.

questions.reviewPearl: Distinguish depersonalization/derealization by the feeling of detachment while maintaining intact reality testing.
questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman is being evaluated for a possible dissociative disorder. She reports feeling "detached from her body," as if she is "an outside observer of her own life." She knows who she is and where she is, but the feeling is distressing. This is characteristic of:
questions.educationalObjective: Distinguish depersonalization/derealization by the feeling of detachment while maintaining intact reality testing.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Depersonalization is the feeling of detachment from oneself (being an outside observer), while derealization is the feeling that the external world is unreal.

metadata.reviewPearl: Distinguish depersonalization/derealization by the feeling of detachment while maintaining intact reality testing.

questions.retrievalTag: Schedule regular monthly visits with a single primary
questions.reviewPearl: The cornerstone of managing Somatic Symptom Disorder is scheduling regular, brief follow-up visits with a single primary care provider to minimize unnecessary medical testing and foster a stable patient-physician rela...
questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with persistent abdominal pain, backaches, and chronic fatigue that have lasted for over a year. She has seen multiple specialists, and exhaustive workups have failed to find a medical explanation for her symptoms. She reports significant distress and spends a large portion of her day researching her symptoms online. Which of the following is the most appropriate first step in the management of this patient?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Refer to a specialized pain clinic

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Schedule regular monthly visits with a single primary care physician

questions.educationalObjective: The cornerstone of managing Somatic Symptom Disorder is scheduling regular, brief follow-up visits with a single primary care provider to minimize unnecessary medical testing and foster a stable patient-physician relationship.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient meets the criteria for Somatic Symptom Disorder, which requires one or more distressing somatic symptoms lasting ≥ 6 months and excessive thoughts or behaviors related to those symptoms. The most important first step in management is establishing a single physician as the primary caregiver and scheduling regular monthly visits. This approach builds a stable therapeutic relationship and minimizes unnecessary, invasive diagnostic testing.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Specialized pain clinic: While the patient has pain, the primary issue is the psychological distress and behavioral response to somatic symptoms, which is better managed in a primary care setting with regular visits.
(C) High-dose SNRI: While medications may be used as adjuncts for specific comorbid symptoms, they are not the "most important first step" compared to establishing stable primary care.
(D) Inpatient hospitalization: This is not indicated for Somatic Symptom Disorder and may actually reinforce the patient's "sick role".

questions.reviewPearl: Differentiate Illness Anxiety Disorder from Somatic Symptom Disorder by the absence or minimal nature of actual physical symptoms; the focus in Illness Anxiety Disorder is the fear of having a disease rather than the...
questions.stem: A 42-year-old male presents to the clinic convinced he has a life-threatening neurological disease despite having no neurological symptoms. He is extremely anxious and frequently checks his reflexes at home. He has had this fear for seven months. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate Illness Anxiety Disorder from Somatic Symptom Disorder by the absence or minimal nature of actual physical symptoms; the focus in Illness Anxiety Disorder is the fear of having a disease rather than the symptoms themselves.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Illness Anxiety Disorder is characterized by a preoccupation with having or acquiring a serious, undiagnosed medical illness. A key differentiating feature from Somatic Symptom Disorder is that somatic symptoms are either not present or are very mild. Both disorders require a duration of at least 6 months.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Conversion disorder: Involves the loss or alteration of motor or sensory function (e.g., blindness, paralysis) usually following a stressor.
(C) Malingering: Involves the intentional production of false or exaggerated symptoms for a clear external or secondary gain.
(D) Somatic symptom disorder: Requires the presence of one or more actual somatic symptoms that are distressing or disruptive to daily life.

metadata.reviewPearl: Differentiate Illness Anxiety Disorder from Somatic Symptom Disorder by the absence or minimal nature of actual physical symptoms; the focus in Illness Anxiety Disorder is the fear of having a disease rather than the...

questions.reviewPearl: Conversion Disorder presents as unexplained neurological symptoms (motor or sensory) often after a stressor; management begins with patient education regarding the nature of the condition.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old male is brought to the clinic after suddenly losing the ability to move his right arm following a highly stressful job termination. Physical examination reveals no neurological basis for the weakness, and the patient's symptoms do not follow an anatomical nerve distribution. What is the recommended first-line management?
questions.educationalObjective: Conversion Disorder presents as unexplained neurological symptoms (motor or sensory) often after a stressor; management begins with patient education regarding the nature of the condition.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is presenting with Conversion Disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder), where psychological stress is manifested as motor or sensory deficits that cannot be explained medically. The first-line management is education about the diagnosis and self-help techniques. CBT is considered a second-line treatment option if education is insufficient.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) CBT: This is a second-line treatment for conversion disorder.
(C) Benzodiazepines: These have no role in treating the primary symptoms of conversion disorder.
(D) Repetitive nerve stimulation: This would be used to evaluate myasthenia gravis, but the clinical history strongly suggests a psychiatric etiology.

metadata.reviewPearl: Conversion Disorder presents as unexplained neurological symptoms (motor or sensory) often after a stressor; management begins with patient education regarding the nature of the condition.

questions.reviewPearl: Identify Malingering by the presence of a clear external incentive and the patient's typically uncooperative behavior during evaluation.
questions.stem: A 29-year-old male with no prior medical history presents to the emergency department claiming he is unable to walk due to severe lower back pain after a workplace incident. He is seeking a formal medical statement for disability compensation. During the evaluation, he is notably uncooperative and refuses to participate in physical therapy. Which of the following conditions is most likely present?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify Malingering by the presence of a clear external incentive and the patient's typically uncooperative behavior during evaluation.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Malingering is the conscious and intentional production or exaggeration of symptoms motivated by an external secondary gain, such as avoiding work or obtaining financial compensation. Patients with malingering are frequently uncooperative during their medical evaluation out of fear of being caught faking.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Conversion disorder: The symptoms are unintentional and not motivated by an external gain.
(B) Factitious disorder: Symptoms are intentionally produced but the motivation is internalto assume the "sick role"rather than for external gain.
(D) Somatic symptom disorder: The symptoms are not intentionally produced, and the patient genuinely believes they have a medical problem.

metadata.reviewPearl: Identify Malingering by the presence of a clear external incentive and the patient's typically uncooperative behavior during evaluation.

questions.reviewPearl: Tourette syndrome requires both motor and vocal tics to be present for >1 year.
questions.stem: A 19-year-old male is evaluated for "strange movements." For the past 14 months, he has had recurrent, sudden, nonrhythmic eye-blinking and shoulder-shrugging. He also reports that he often blurts out "barking" sounds in class, which he cannot control. He notes these movements are preceded by an "itchy" sensation in his shoulders that goes away once he shrugs. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Tourette syndrome requires both motor and vocal tics to be present for >1 year.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Tourette syndrome is a childhood-onset disorder characterized by both multiple motor tics and at least one vocal tic persisting for more than one year. Tics are typically preceded by a premonitory urge (e.g., the itchy sensation) and are exacerbated by stress.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Chronic motor tic disorder: Used if only motor tics or only vocal tics are present, but not both.
(D) Transient tic disorder: Used if tics have been present for less than one year.

metadata.reviewPearl: Tourette syndrome requires both motor and vocal tics to be present for >1 year.

questions.retrievalTag: Fluid restriction at night and positive reinforcement
questions.reviewPearl: The initial management of nocturnal enuresis in children ≥ 5 years old involves behavioral interventions such as fluid restriction, scheduled voids, and positive reinforcement.
questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician by his mother because he still "wets the bed" three nights a week. He has never achieved a consistent period of nighttime dryness. Physical examination and urinalysis are normal. What is the next best step in the management of this patient?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Fluid restriction at night and positive reinforcement

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Reassurance that this is normal behavior for his age

questions.educationalObjective: The initial management of nocturnal enuresis in children ≥ 5 years old involves behavioral interventions such as fluid restriction, scheduled voids, and positive reinforcement.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has primary nocturnal enuresis. In a child ≥ 5 years old where organic causes have been ruled out, management begins with lifestyle modifications and behavioral training, including fluid restriction at night, timed voiding, and positive reinforcement.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Desmopressin: Typically reserved for children ≥ 7 years old or those who have failed non-pharmacological interventions.
(D) Reassurance: While enuresis is common, it is considered pathological and diagnosable once the child is ≥ 5 years old.

metadata.retrievalTag: Fluid restriction at night and positive reinforcement
metadata.reviewPearl: The initial management of nocturnal enuresis in children ≥ 5 years old involves behavioral interventions such as fluid restriction, scheduled voids, and positive reinforcement.

questions.reviewPearl: Desmopressin is the preferred first-line medication for nocturnal enuresis when behavioral therapies fail.
questions.stem: A 9-year-old child with persistent bedwetting has failed to improve with fluid restriction and the use of an enuresis alarm. The parents are seeking a pharmacological option to help during an upcoming overnight camping trip. Which of the following medications is the preferred first-line choice?
questions.educationalObjective: Desmopressin is the preferred first-line medication for nocturnal enuresis when behavioral therapies fail.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Desmopressin is the first-line pharmacotherapy for nocturnal enuresis. It is an analog of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) that decreases urine production during sleep. It is preferred over imipramine due to a significantly more favorable side effect profile.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Imipramine: A tricyclic antidepressant that is considered second-line due to its adverse side effects.

metadata.reviewPearl: Desmopressin is the preferred first-line medication for nocturnal enuresis when behavioral therapies fail.

questions.reviewPearl: Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant first-line alternative for ADHD, particularly in patients with a history of substance use disorder.
questions.stem: A 32-year-old male with a history of alcohol use disorder and stimulant misuse presents for the evaluation of symptoms consistent with ADHD. The physician wishes to avoid medications with a potential for misuse. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy for this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant first-line alternative for ADHD, particularly in patients with a history of substance use disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: While stimulants like methylphenidate are the most effective treatment for ADHD, they have a potential for misuse and addiction. In patients with a history of substance use disorder, atomoxetine (a non-stimulant selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor) is the treatment of choice.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (C) Methylphenidate: A first-line stimulant for ADHD but generally avoided or used with extreme caution in patients with active or recent substance misuse.

metadata.reviewPearl: Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant first-line alternative for ADHD, particularly in patients with a history of substance use disorder.

questions.reviewPearl: Encopresis is defined by the inappropriate passage of stool in a child ≥ 4 years old.
questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the clinic because he has been repeatedly "soiling his pants" with feces for the past four months. The mother states this happens during the day at school. He has no medical conditions. According to the DSM-5, what is the minimum age required to diagnose this condition?
questions.educationalObjective: Encopresis is defined by the inappropriate passage of stool in a child ≥ 4 years old.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The diagnosis of pathological encopresis (repeated passage of feces into inappropriate places) cannot be made until the child is at least 4 years old, or at an equivalent developmental level.

metadata.reviewPearl: Encopresis is defined by the inappropriate passage of stool in a child ≥ 4 years old.

questions.reviewPearl: Conduct disorder involves a pervasive pattern of violating social norms and the rights of others in a patient < 18 years old.
questions.stem: An 11-year-old boy is evaluated for aggressive behavior. Over the past year, he has been caught setting fire to a neighbor's shed and has been found harming local stray cats. He frequently steals money from his parents' wallets and has been suspended for fighting. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Conduct disorder involves a pervasive pattern of violating social norms and the rights of others in a patient < 18 years old.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Conduct disorder is a childhood disorder characterized by repetitive behaviors that violate the basic rights of others or societal norms, including aggression to people/animals and destruction of property. If these behaviors persist after age 18, the diagnosis is reclassified as Antisocial Personality Disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Antisocial personality disorder: Requires the patient to be ≥ 18 years old.

(D) Oppositional defiant disorder: Characterized by defiance and hostility toward authority figures but does not typically include the severe aggression or property destruction seen in conduct disorder.

metadata.reviewPearl: Conduct disorder involves a pervasive pattern of violating social norms and the rights of others in a patient < 18 years old.

questions.retrievalTag: Report the suspected abuse to child protective services
questions.reviewPearl: Suspected child abuse must be reported immediately to CPS; the physician should also ensure the child is in a safe location.
questions.stem: A 6-year-old child is brought to the clinic for a routine check-up. The physician notices several circular, uniform-depth burns on the child's buttocks with sharp lines of demarcation. The mother states the child "fell into the bathtub." Which of the following is the most appropriate first action by the physician?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Interview the child alone with the mother's permission

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Report the suspected abuse to child protective services (CPS)

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Reassure the mother and provide burn care instructions

questions.educationalObjective: Suspected child abuse must be reported immediately to CPS; the physician should also ensure the child is in a safe location.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: If a physician suspects child abuse based on injuries that are inconsistent with the provided history (e.g., intentional scald injuries with sharp demarcation), they are legally obligated to report the suspicion immediately to Child Protective Services (CPS). Ensuring the child's safety is the top priority.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Interview child alone: While this can be done, the mandatory first legal and protective step is notifying CPS.

metadata.retrievalTag: Report the suspected abuse to child protective services
metadata.reviewPearl: Suspected child abuse must be reported immediately to CPS; the physician should also ensure the child is in a safe location.

questions.reviewPearl: Shaken Baby Syndrome involves acceleration-deceleration forces resulting in the classic findings of subdural hematoma and retinal hemorrhages.
questions.stem: An infant is brought to the emergency department in a comatose state. A CT scan of the head reveals a subdural hematoma. Fundoscopic examination reveals retinal hemorrhages. What is the most likely mechanism of this injury?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Blunt force trauma to the skull

questions.educationalObjective: Shaken Baby Syndrome involves acceleration-deceleration forces resulting in the classic findings of subdural hematoma and retinal hemorrhages.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The combination of subdural hematoma and retinal hemorrhages in an infant is highly suggestive of Shaken Baby Syndrome (abusive head trauma). This is an example of an acceleration-deceleration injury, which shears the fragile bridging veins. It is distinct from blunt head trauma.

metadata.reviewPearl: Shaken Baby Syndrome involves acceleration-deceleration forces resulting in the classic findings of subdural hematoma and retinal hemorrhages.

questions.reviewPearl: Identify Delirium by its acute onset, fluctuating level of consciousness, and typical occurrence following medical illness or surgery in the elderly.
questions.stem: A 78-year-old man underwent hip replacement surgery three days ago [uploaded:ANKI 1.txt]. He has become increasingly agitated and intermittently believes the hospital staff are "enemy soldiers" [uploaded:ANKI 1.txt]. His level of orientation fluctuates throughout the day, and his symptoms are significantly worse in the evening [uploaded:ANKI 1.txt]. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify Delirium by its acute onset, fluctuating level of consciousness, and typical occurrence following medical illness or surgery in the elderly.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This patient is presenting with Delirium, an acute, fluctuating disturbance in consciousness and attention common in elderly patients after surgery. The worsening of symptoms in the evening is a classic phenomenon known as "sundowning".

metadata.reviewPearl: Identify Delirium by its acute onset, fluctuating level of consciousness, and typical occurrence following medical illness or surgery in the elderly.

questions.reviewPearl: HIV-associated dementia typically manifests with subcortical symptoms, including cognitive slowing and motor impairment.
questions.stem: A 44-year-old male with a history of HIV (last CD4+ count 150/mm�) is brought in due to progressive cognitive decline [uploaded:ANKI 1.txt]. His husband notes that his thinking has become very "sluggish" and he has developed slow, jerky movements in his arms [uploaded:ANKI 1.txt]. What is the most likely focus of his neurocognitive dysfunction?
questions.educationalObjective: HIV-associated dementia typically manifests with subcortical symptoms, including cognitive slowing and motor impairment.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: HIV-associated dementia is primarily characterized by subcortical dysfunction, which leads to slowed thinking (bradyphrenia) and motor abnormalities like slow or jerky movements.

metadata.reviewPearl: HIV-associated dementia typically manifests with subcortical symptoms, including cognitive slowing and motor impairment.

questions.reviewPearl: CBT is the first-line treatment for chronic insomnia; pharmacological treatments are generally avoided for long-term use.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman complains of difficulty falling asleep and staying asleep for more than 4 nights a week over the past 4 months. She reports that this is causing significant daytime fatigue. What is the treatment of choice for this condition?
questions.educationalObjective: CBT is the first-line treatment for chronic insomnia; pharmacological treatments are generally avoided for long-term use.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient meets the criteria for Chronic Insomnia (≥ 3 nights/week for ≥ 3 months). CBT is the first-line therapy for chronic insomnia. Sedative-hypnotics should only be used sparingly for short-term relief.

metadata.reviewPearl: CBT is the first-line treatment for chronic insomnia; pharmacological treatments are generally avoided for long-term use.

questions.reviewPearl: Recognize REM Sleep Behavior Disorder as a potential early indicator of alpha-synuclein neurodegenerative disorders like Parkinson's disease.
questions.stem: A 68-year-old male is brought to the clinic because he "acts out his dreams". His wife reports he has accidentally kicked her in his sleep while yelling at imaginary intruders in his dream. He becomes fully alert immediately upon awakening. This condition is most strongly associated with the later development of which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize REM Sleep Behavior Disorder as a potential early indicator of alpha-synuclein neurodegenerative disorders like Parkinson's disease.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has REM Sleep Behavior Disorder, characterized by dream enactment due to the absence of normal REM muscle atonia. In older patients, this is a known prodromal sign of neurodegeneration, specifically Parkinson's disease or Dementia with Lewy Bodies.

metadata.reviewPearl: Recognize REM Sleep Behavior Disorder as a potential early indicator of alpha-synuclein neurodegenerative disorders like Parkinson's disease.

These symptoms resolve almost immediately once her menses begins, but they interfere with her job performance. What is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Record symptoms in a menstrual diary for two cycles

questions.educationalObjective: Diagnosis of PMS and PMDD requires the use of a symptom diary to confirm that symptoms occur cyclically in relation to menses.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient likely has Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder (PMDD). To confirm the diagnosis, patients should record their symptoms in a menstrual diary for at least two cycles to demonstrate the cyclic nature of the symptoms (occurring during the luteal phase and resolving in the follicular phase).

questions.retrievalTag: It is more likely to be enduring as
questions.reviewPearl: Gender dysphoria that persists into adolescence and puberty has a high likelihood of being enduring.
questions.stem: A 14-year-old who was assigned female at birth reports an intense, persistent desire to be a boy. The patient experiences significant distress when going through puberty and feels rejected by peers. At this stage of development, which of the following is true regarding the patient's condition?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: It is highly likely to resolve spontaneously before adulthood

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: It is more likely to be enduring as it has persisted into adolescence

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Hormone therapy should be initiated immediately to prevent distress

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: No intervention should be considered until the patient is 18 years old

questions.educationalObjective: Gender dysphoria that persists into adolescence and puberty has a high likelihood of being enduring.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: While gender dysphoria in early childhood does not always persist, it is more likely to be enduring when experienced through adolescence and into puberty. Hormone-suppression therapy can be considered starting at Tanner stage 2 to delay puberty, but only after a complete specialist assessment.

metadata.retrievalTag: It is more likely to be enduring as
metadata.reviewPearl: Gender dysphoria that persists into adolescence and puberty has a high likelihood of being enduring.

questions.retrievalTag: Emotionally withdrawn and unresponsive to comfort
questions.reviewPearl: Recognize Reactive Attachment Disorder as a response to severe early neglect, characterized by emotional withdrawal and an inability to seek or respond to comfort.
questions.stem: Which of the following describes the core clinical feature of Reactive Attachment Disorder in a child who has suffered significant neglect or deprivation of affection?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Emotionally withdrawn and unresponsive to comfort

questions.educationalObjective: Recognize Reactive Attachment Disorder as a response to severe early neglect, characterized by emotional withdrawal and an inability to seek or respond to comfort.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Reactive Attachment Disorder occurs in children who have experienced long-term deprivation of affection; it is characterized by the child being emotionally withdrawn and unresponsive to comfort. This is contrasted with Disinhibited Social Engagement Disorder, where children are overly familiar with strangers.

metadata.retrievalTag: Emotionally withdrawn and unresponsive to comfort
metadata.reviewPearl: Recognize Reactive Attachment Disorder as a response to severe early neglect, characterized by emotional withdrawal and an inability to seek or respond to comfort.

questions.reviewPearl: The minimum duration for a major depressive episode is 2 weeks.
questions.stem: A 24-year-old patient with Major Depressive Disorder reports feeling "empty," has a history of suicide attempts, and describes a weight loss of 15 pounds over the last three weeks [uploaded:ANKI 1.txt]. They report a loss of interest in all previously enjoyed activities [uploaded:ANKI 1.txt]. According to the diagnostic criteria, what is the minimum duration of symptoms required to diagnose a major depressive episode?
questions.educationalObjective: The minimum duration for a major depressive episode is 2 weeks.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: A diagnosis of Major Depressive Disorder requires at least 5 "SIG E CAPS" symptoms (one of which must be depressed mood or anhedonia) lasting for at least 2 weeks.

metadata.reviewPearl: The minimum duration for a major depressive episode is 2 weeks.

questions.reviewPearl: Wernicke encephalopathy is characterized by confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia due to thiamine deficiency, which causes bilateral damage to the mammillary bodies.
questions.stem: A 58-year-old male is brought to the emergency department by his sister, who is concerned about his "wobbliness" and confusion. The patient has a 30-year history of heavy alcohol use and currently appears disoriented to time and place. On physical examination, he exhibits a wide-based gait and nystagmus. Which of the following areas of the brain is most likely damaged in this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Wernicke encephalopathy is characterized by confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia due to thiamine deficiency, which causes bilateral damage to the mammillary bodies.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is presenting with the classic triad of Wernicke encephalopathy: confusion, ataxia, and ocular abnormalities (nystagmus/ophthalmoplegia). This condition is caused by thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency and results in bilateral damage to the mammillary bodies and the dorsomedial nucleus of the thalamus.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A, B): While the hippocampus and amygdala are involved in memory and emotion, they are not the primary sites of damage in Wernicke encephalopathy.
(D) Substantia nigra: Damage to the substantia nigra is the hallmark of Parkinson's disease, not Wernicke encephalopathy.

metadata.reviewPearl: Wernicke encephalopathy is characterized by confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia due to thiamine deficiency, which causes bilateral damage to the mammillary bodies.

questions.reviewPearl: Always administer thiamine prior to or along with glucose in malnourished or alcoholic patients to avoid precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy.
questions.stem: A 45-year-old homeless man is admitted for severe malnourishment and alcohol withdrawal. To prevent the precipitation of an acute neurological syndrome, in which order should intravenous fluids and vitamins be administered?
questions.educationalObjective: Always administer thiamine prior to or along with glucose in malnourished or alcoholic patients to avoid precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: In a thiamine-deficient patient (e.g., those with chronic alcoholism or malnourishment), giving glucose before or without thiamine can iatrogenically precipitate Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome. Thiamine is a necessary cofactor for glucose metabolism; without it, glucose administration increases metabolic demand and worsens cellular damage in the brain.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A, D): Administering glucose first can trigger acute encephalopathy in B1-deficient individuals.

metadata.reviewPearl: Always administer thiamine prior to or along with glucose in malnourished or alcoholic patients to avoid precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy.

questions.reviewPearl: Korsakoff syndrome involves permanent memory loss and confabulation due to damage to the dorsomedial nucleus of the thalamus and the mammillary bodies.
questions.stem: A 62-year-old male with chronic alcoholism is being treated for a recent episode of encephalopathy. While his confusion has improved, he is now unable to form new memories and frequently fills in gaps in his memory with fabricated stories that he believes to be true. Damage to which of the following thalamic nuclei is most likely responsible for this memory impairment?
questions.educationalObjective: Korsakoff syndrome involves permanent memory loss and confabulation due to damage to the dorsomedial nucleus of the thalamus and the mammillary bodies.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has developed Korsakoff syndrome, a chronic complication of thiamine deficiency characterized by anterograde amnesia and confabulation (fabricated memories). This is associated with periventricular hemorrhagic lesions in the dorsomedial nucleus of the thalamus and the mammillary bodies.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) LGN: Relays visual information.
(C) MGN: Relays auditory information.
(D) VPL: Relays sensory information from the body to the cortex.

metadata.reviewPearl: Korsakoff syndrome involves permanent memory loss and confabulation due to damage to the dorsomedial nucleus of the thalamus and the mammillary bodies.

questions.reviewPearl: A diagnosis of substance use disorder requires ≥ 2 symptoms related to substance use occurring within a 1-year period.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old female is evaluated for a suspected substance use disorder. According to the DSM-5, what is the minimum number of criteria that must be met within a one-year period to qualify for a diagnosis of substance use disorder?
questions.educationalObjective: A diagnosis of substance use disorder requires ≥ 2 symptoms related to substance use occurring within a 1-year period.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Substance use disorder is defined by a pattern of use leading to clinically significant impairment or distress, manifested by at least 2 specific signs (e.g., tolerance, withdrawal, craving, failure to fulfill obligations) occurring within a 12-month (1-year) period.

metadata.reviewPearl: A diagnosis of substance use disorder requires ≥ 2 symptoms related to substance use occurring within a 1-year period.

questions.reviewPearl: Delirium tremens presents 24 days after cessation of alcohol use with agitation, autonomic hyperactivity, and delirium.
questions.stem: A 44-year-old male is admitted to the hospital for an emergency appendectomy. Three days after admission, he becomes extremely agitated, develops a coarse tremor, and begins having visual hallucinations of "giant spiders" on the wall. His heart rate is 120/min and blood pressure is 165/100 mmHg. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Delirium tremens presents 24 days after cessation of alcohol use with agitation, autonomic hyperactivity, and delirium.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is experiencing delirium tremens (DTs), the most severe form of alcohol withdrawal. It typically occurs 4896 hours (24 days) after the last drink and is characterized by autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension), altered sensorium (delirium), and hallucinations.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Alcoholic hallucinosis: Occurs earlier (1224 hours) and is characterized by stable vital signs and an intact sensorium.

metadata.reviewPearl: Delirium tremens presents 24 days after cessation of alcohol use with agitation, autonomic hyperactivity, and delirium.

questions.reviewPearl: Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for managing the symptoms and life-threatening complications of alcohol withdrawal.
questions.stem: Which of the following is the preferred pharmacological treatment for a patient experiencing delirium tremens, especially in a hospital setting where intravenous access is available?
questions.educationalObjective: Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for managing the symptoms and life-threatening complications of alcohol withdrawal.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Benzodiazepines, such as lorazepam, are the treatment of choice for alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens. They help achieve sedation and stabilize autonomic hyperactivity. In patients with hepatic disease, short-acting agents like lorazepam are preferred.

metadata.reviewPearl: Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for managing the symptoms and life-threatening complications of alcohol withdrawal.

questions.reviewPearl: Distinguish alcoholic hallucinosis from DTs by the stable vital signs and clear sensorium in hallucinosis.
questions.stem: A 35-year-old male presents with visual hallucinations 16 hours after his last drink. He is oriented to person, place, and time, and his vital signs are normal. He has a history of heavy alcohol use but has never had a seizure. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Distinguish alcoholic hallucinosis from DTs by the stable vital signs and clear sensorium in hallucinosis.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Alcoholic hallucinosis is characterized by visual, auditory, or tactile hallucinations that develop within 1224 hours of abstinence. Unlike delirium tremens, the patient's vital signs remain stable and their sensorium is clear (intact orientation).

metadata.reviewPearl: Distinguish alcoholic hallucinosis from DTs by the stable vital signs and clear sensorium in hallucinosis.

questions.reviewPearl: Cocaine use can lead to myocardial ischemia via coronary artery vasospasm.
questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department for severe substernal chest pain. She reports she was at a party where she used an "intoxicant." An EKG shows transient ST-segment elevations. What is the likely mechanism of this patient's cardiac findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Cocaine use can lead to myocardial ischemia via coronary artery vasospasm.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is likely experiencing cocaine toxicity, which can induce coronary vasospasm. This results in myocardial ischemia and can manifest as Prinzmetal (vasospastic) angina.

metadata.reviewPearl: Cocaine use can lead to myocardial ischemia via coronary artery vasospasm.

questions.reviewPearl: Avoid beta-blockers in acute cocaine intoxication to prevent unopposed alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction.
questions.stem: Which of the following medications is strictly contraindicated in the acute management of a patient with cocaine-induced hypertension and chest pain?
questions.educationalObjective: Avoid beta-blockers in acute cocaine intoxication to prevent unopposed alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Selective beta-blockers (e.g., propranolol) are contraindicated in acute cocaine intoxication. Blocking beta_2 receptors leads to unopposed alpha-1 stimulation, which causes severe vasoconstriction and can worsen hypertension and coronary vasospasm. IV benzodiazepines are the preferred treatment to alleviate psychomotor agitation and sympathomimetic effects.

metadata.reviewPearl: Avoid beta-blockers in acute cocaine intoxication to prevent unopposed alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction.

questions.reviewPearl: Cocaine withdrawal presents as a "crash" with depression, fatigue, and increased appetite.
questions.stem: A 29-year-old male who has been using cocaine daily for the past month suddenly stops. Two days later, he presents with severe depression, fatigue, hypersomnia, and reports having vivid, unpleasant dreams. He has an increased appetite. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Cocaine withdrawal presents as a "crash" with depression, fatigue, and increased appetite.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is experiencing a cocaine "crash" or withdrawal. This syndrome is characterized by dysphoric mood (depression), fatigue, hypersomnia, increased appetite, and vivid dreaming following the cessation of heavy, prolonged cocaine use.

metadata.reviewPearl: Cocaine withdrawal presents as a "crash" with depression, fatigue, and increased appetite.

questions.reviewPearl: Bath salt intoxication mimics amphetamines but features a prolonged duration of effect (up to one week) and is often not detected on routine tox screens.
questions.stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the ED after being found highly combative and psychotic. His urine toxicology is negative for PCP, cocaine, and amphetamines. He remains psychotic and agitated for six days despite supportive care. Which of the following substances is the most likely cause of his prolonged symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Bath salt intoxication mimics amphetamines but features a prolonged duration of effect (up to one week) and is often not detected on routine tox screens.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Synthetic cathinones ("bath salts") produce symptoms similar to PCP and amphetamines (agitation, psychosis, SNS stimulation) but have a much longer duration of action, potentially lasting up to a week. Additionally, many bath salts are not detected on standard urine toxicology screens.

metadata.reviewPearl: Bath salt intoxication mimics amphetamines but features a prolonged duration of effect (up to one week) and is often not detected on routine tox screens.

questions.reviewPearl: Conjunctival injection and increased appetite are key physical findings of marijuana intoxication.
questions.stem: A 19-year-old college student is evaluated in the student health center. He appears mildly paranoid and reports that "time seems to be moving slowly." Physical examination reveals conjunctival injection, tachycardia, and a dry mouth. He also mentions he is "extremely hungry." What is the most likely intoxicant?
questions.educationalObjective: Conjunctival injection and increased appetite are key physical findings of marijuana intoxication.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Marijuana (cannabis) intoxication typically presents with euphoria, anxiety, perception of slowed time, increased appetite, and conjunctival injection (red eyes). Paranoid delusions and dry mouth can also occur.

metadata.reviewPearl: Conjunctival injection and increased appetite are key physical findings of marijuana intoxication.

questions.reviewPearl: Inhalant abuse often presents with a perioral/perinasal rash and transient CNS depression.
questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy is found unconscious in his bedroom. Upon awakening in the ED, he is drowsy and complains of a headache. Physical examination is significant for a rash around his mouth. A routine toxicology screen is negative. What is the most likely cause of his symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Inhalant abuse often presents with a perioral/perinasal rash and transient CNS depression.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The presence of a perioral rash ("glue sniffer's rash") in an adolescent with a negative toxicology screen and acute neurological changes (unconsciousness, headache) is classic for inhalant abuse (e.g., glue, toluene).

metadata.reviewPearl: Inhalant abuse often presents with a perioral/perinasal rash and transient CNS depression.

questions.reviewPearl: The Reitan trail test is a sensitive tool for detecting subtle alterations in mental status.
questions.stem: A 70-year-old male with chronic liver disease is evaluated for subtle cognitive changes. Which of the following tests, involving a timed connect-the-numbers exercise, is most useful for detecting these subtle mental status changes?
questions.educationalObjective: The Reitan trail test is a sensitive tool for detecting subtle alterations in mental status.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The Reitan trail test is a timed connect-the-numbers test used specifically to detect subtle changes in mental status and cognitive processing.

metadata.reviewPearl: The Reitan trail test is a sensitive tool for detecting subtle alterations in mental status.

questions.reviewPearl: Physicians assess clinical capacity; judges determine legal competence.
questions.stem: A physician is discussing the treatment of a 75-year-old patient with her family. The physician notes that while the patient currently lacks the ability to make a specific medical decision, only a judge can officially declare the patient to be "incompetent." Which of the following terms describes the clinical assessment of a patient's decision-making ability performed by a physician?
questions.educationalObjective: Physicians assess clinical capacity; judges determine legal competence.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Capacity is a clinical term determined by a physician, whereas competence is a legal term decided only by a judge.

metadata.reviewPearl: Physicians assess clinical capacity; judges determine legal competence.

questions.reviewPearl: Mild intellectual disability corresponds to an IQ range of 5570.
questions.stem: A 20-year-old patient is found to have an IQ score of 60. According to the standard classifications for intellectual disability, which of the following categories does this score fall into?
questions.educationalObjective: Mild intellectual disability corresponds to an IQ range of 5570.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Intellectual disability is categorized by IQ ranges: 5570 is mild, 4055 is moderate, and 2540 is severe.

metadata.reviewPearl: Mild intellectual disability corresponds to an IQ range of 5570.

questions.reviewPearl: Reaction formation involves converting an unacceptable impulse into its behavioral opposite.
questions.stem: A 35-year-old man who is notoriously xenophobic and outspoken about his dislike of foreigners is observed to be extremely nice and welcoming to a group of international tourists at his workplace. Which defense mechanism is this patient utilizing?
questions.educationalObjective: Reaction formation involves converting an unacceptable impulse into its behavioral opposite.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Reaction formation is a defense mechanism where a person adopts a behavior that is the exact opposite of their true, unacceptable feelings or impulses (e.g., being extremely nice to foreigners while actually being xenophobic).

metadata.reviewPearl: Reaction formation involves converting an unacceptable impulse into its behavioral opposite.

questions.reviewPearl: Denial involves the refusal to accept or acknowledge a distressing reality.
questions.stem: A 69-year-old man is diagnosed with metastatic lung cancer. When his doctor explains the prognosis, the patient replies, "It's just a bad cold. All I need is some hot tea and rest, and I'll be fine." Which defense mechanism is most evident?
questions.educationalObjective: Denial involves the refusal to accept or acknowledge a distressing reality.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Denial is the defense mechanism of avoiding the awareness of a painful reality (e.g., treating metastatic cancer as a simple cold).

metadata.reviewPearl: Denial involves the refusal to accept or acknowledge a distressing reality.

questions.reviewPearl: ADLs include basic self-care like dressing and bathing; IADLs involve more complex life skills.
questions.stem: In assessing a geriatric patient's functional status, which of the following activities is classified as an Activity of Daily Living (ADL) rather than an Instrumental Activity of Daily Living (IADL)?
questions.educationalObjective: ADLs include basic self-care like dressing and bathing; IADLs involve more complex life skills.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: ADLs represent the basic, essential self-care tasks, such as walking, bathing, dressing, and eating. IADLs are more advanced tasks required for independent living, such as managing finances or shopping.

metadata.reviewPearl: ADLs include basic self-care like dressing and bathing; IADLs involve more complex life skills.

questions.reviewPearl: Diminished or absent DTRs and decreased upward gaze can be normal physiological changes in the elderly.
questions.stem: Which of the following is considered a normal finding in the physical examination of a healthy elderly patient?

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Short-term memory loss of recent events

questions.educationalObjective: Diminished or absent DTRs and decreased upward gaze can be normal physiological changes in the elderly.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: An absence of deep tendon reflexes (DTRs) can be a normal finding in healthy elderly individuals. Additionally, a decrease in upward gaze is also considered a normal part of aging.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B, D): Being unable to operate appliances or recall recent events are signs of pathological cognitive decline, not normal aging.

metadata.reviewPearl: Diminished or absent DTRs and decreased upward gaze can be normal physiological changes in the elderly.

questions.reviewPearl: Getting lost in familiar surroundings or an inability to recall recent important events are signs of dementia.
questions.stem: Which of the following findings in an 80-year-old patient most strongly suggests a pathological condition rather than normal aging?
questions.educationalObjective: Getting lost in familiar surroundings or an inability to recall recent important events are signs of dementia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Getting lost in familiar territory is not a normal part of aging and is a significant indicator of a neurocognitive disorder (dementia). Similarly, being unable to operate common appliances or recall important recent conversations is abnormal.

metadata.reviewPearl: Getting lost in familiar surroundings or an inability to recall recent important events are signs of dementia.

questions.reviewPearl: Reduced central serotonergic activity is linked to increased aggression and impulsive behavior.
questions.stem: Laboratory studies have shown that low levels of which neurotransmitter in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) are associated with increased impulsiveness and aggression?
questions.educationalObjective: Reduced central serotonergic activity is linked to increased aggression and impulsive behavior.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Low levels of serotonin in the CSF have been shown to be associated with impulsiveness and aggression. High levels of dopamine may also contribute to these behaviors.

metadata.reviewPearl: Reduced central serotonergic activity is linked to increased aggression and impulsive behavior.

questions.reviewPearl: Serotonin is primarily synthesized in the raphe nuclei.
questions.stem: The majority of serotonin production in the central nervous system occurs in which of the following structures?
questions.educationalObjective: Serotonin is primarily synthesized in the raphe nuclei.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The raphe nuclei are the primary sites of serotonin production in the CNS.

metadata.reviewPearl: Serotonin is primarily synthesized in the raphe nuclei.

questions.reviewPearl: CBT is the treatment of choice for addressing cognitive distortions and maladaptive behaviors.
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman is referred to psychotherapy for help with maladaptive thought patterns, such as "catastrophizing" minor setbacks at work and "negatively interpreting" the actions of her colleagues. Which of the following types of therapy is most appropriate for this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: CBT is the treatment of choice for addressing cognitive distortions and maladaptive behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: CBT is a standardized modality of psychotherapy that specifically targets persistent maladaptive thought patterns and behaviors. It involves identifying and changing cognitive distortions like catastrophizing and overgeneralization.

metadata.reviewPearl: CBT is the treatment of choice for addressing cognitive distortions and maladaptive behaviors.

questions.reviewPearl: Family therapy is an effective adjunctive treatment for schizophrenia that reduces relapse rates by improving the home environment.
questions.stem: A 26-year-old male with schizophrenia has been hospitalized multiple times due to medication non-compliance following stressful interactions with his parents. Which type of intervention has been shown to decrease the risk of relapse by minimizing conflict and educating relatives about the disorder?
questions.educationalObjective: Family therapy is an effective adjunctive treatment for schizophrenia that reduces relapse rates by improving the home environment.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: In patients with schizophrenia, family therapy helps reduce the risk of relapse by minimizing conflict and stress at home and educating family members on what to expect from the illness.

metadata.reviewPearl: Family therapy is an effective adjunctive treatment for schizophrenia that reduces relapse rates by improving the home environment.

questions.reviewPearl: Interpersonal psychotherapy targets relationship conflicts and social stressors to treat depression.
questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with moderate depression reports that her symptoms are worsened by frequent conflicts with her partner and feelings of isolation. Which type of psychotherapy specifically focuses on the link between depressive symptoms and these relationship stressors?
questions.educationalObjective: Interpersonal psychotherapy targets relationship conflicts and social stressors to treat depression.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Interpersonal psychotherapy is an evidence-based therapy for depression that focuses on the interplay between depressive symptoms and interpersonal stressors (e.g., relationship conflicts).

metadata.reviewPearl: Interpersonal psychotherapy targets relationship conflicts and social stressors to treat depression.

questions.reviewPearl: DBT is the gold-standard psychotherapy for managing the core symptoms of Borderline Personality Disorder.
questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with Borderline Personality Disorder is referred for a specialized form of therapy that targets emotional dysregulation and self-destructive behaviors. What is the name of this therapy?
questions.educationalObjective: DBT is the gold-standard psychotherapy for managing the core symptoms of Borderline Personality Disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Dialectical behavioral therapy (DBT) was developed specifically for Borderline Personality Disorder. It targets emotional dysregulation and self-destructive or suicidal behaviors.

metadata.reviewPearl: DBT is the gold-standard psychotherapy for managing the core symptoms of Borderline Personality Disorder.

questions.reviewPearl: Biofeedback allows patients to gain voluntary control over autonomic physiological functions to treat various disorders.
questions.stem: A patient with migraines and tension headaches is taught to use signals from her body, such as heart rate and muscle tension, to mentally control her physiological responses to pain. What is the name of this therapeutic modality?
questions.educationalObjective: Biofeedback allows patients to gain voluntary control over autonomic physiological functions to treat various disorders.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Biofeedback involves using signals from the body (e.g., heart rate, blood pressure, muscle tension) as indicators of emotional distress to help patients learn to mentally control their physiological reactions. It is used for conditions like migraines, agoraphobia, and hypertension.

metadata.reviewPearl: Biofeedback allows patients to gain voluntary control over autonomic physiological functions to treat various disorders.

questions.reviewPearl: The Freud's Ego is responsible for reality testing and mediating between internal drives and the external world.
questions.stem: According to Freud's structural model of the psyche, which of the following components serves as the mediator between instinctual urges and the external environment, utilizing reality testing to distinguish fantasy from reality?
questions.educationalObjective: The Freud's Ego is responsible for reality testing and mediating between internal drives and the external world.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The Ego serves as the mediator between the Id (unconscious instinctual urges) and the Superego (moral conscience), using reality testing to control urges and adapt to the external environment. Problems with reality testing occur in psychotic individuals.

metadata.reviewPearl: The Freud's Ego is responsible for reality testing and mediating between internal drives and the external world.

questions.reviewPearl: Undoing is a defense mechanism characterized by performing an action to symbolically counteract an unacceptable impulse or thought.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old man who feels a brief, intrusive fantasy of "killing his wife" immediately decides to take her car to a mechanic for a complete safety checkup and maintenance. Which immature defense mechanism is most clearly demonstrated?
questions.educationalObjective: Undoing is a defense mechanism characterized by performing an action to symbolically counteract an unacceptable impulse or thought.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Undoing is an immature defense mechanism where an individual attempts to reverse or "nullify" an unacceptable thought or situation by adopting a new, compensatory behavior (e.g., the husband who fantasizes about harm and then obsessively ensures his wife's safety). Controlling involves regulating the external environment to relieve anxiety.

metadata.reviewPearl: Undoing is a defense mechanism characterized by performing an action to symbolically counteract an unacceptable impulse or thought.

questions.reviewPearl: Wernicke encephalopathy is defined by the triad of confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia due to thiamine deficiency.
questions.stem: A 55-year-old male with a long-standing history of heavy alcohol use is brought to the emergency department for a "triad" of symptoms including confusion, a wide-based gait, and inability to move his eyes laterally. Which of the following vitamin deficiencies is responsible for this condition?
questions.educationalObjective: Wernicke encephalopathy is defined by the triad of confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia due to thiamine deficiency.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is presenting with the classic triad of Wernicke encephalopathy: Confusion, Ataxia, and Nystagmus/Ophthalmoplegia (mnemonic: CAN O'beer). This condition is caused by a deficiency in thiamine (Vitamin B1) and is most commonly seen in patients with chronic alcohol use or severe malnourishment.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Niacin: Deficiency causes pellagra (diarrhea, dermatitis, dementia).
(B) Pyridoxine: Deficiency can cause peripheral neuropathy and sideroblastic anemia.
(D) Vitamin B12: Deficiency causes subacute combined degeneration and megaloblastic anemia.

metadata.reviewPearl: Wernicke encephalopathy is defined by the triad of confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia due to thiamine deficiency.

questions.reviewPearl: Korsakoff syndrome is characterized by permanent memory loss and confabulation.
questions.stem: A 62-year-old chronic alcoholic is recovering from an acute episode of confusion and nystagmus. While his orientation has improved, he is now unable to remember new information and frequently provides fabricated stories about his past that he appears to believe are true. Which of the following symptoms is most characteristic of this patient's current condition?
questions.educationalObjective: Korsakoff syndrome is characterized by permanent memory loss and confabulation.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has developed Korsakoff syndrome, a chronic neurological condition following Wernicke encephalopathy. Key features include permanent memory loss (anterograde amnesia), personality changes, and confabulationthe creation of fabricated memories to fill gaps in recent memory.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Auditory hallucinations: More typical of primary psychotic disorders or alcoholic hallucinosis.
(C, D): These are not standard features of Korsakoff syndrome.

metadata.reviewPearl: Korsakoff syndrome is characterized by permanent memory loss and confabulation.

questions.reviewPearl: Identify cocaine intoxication by its sympathomimetic effects: hypertension, tachycardia, and mydriasis.
questions.stem: A 24-year-old male is brought to the emergency department for severe agitation. He was found at a nightclub yelling at patrons and appearing highly energetic. Physical examination reveals a blood pressure of 185/110 mmHg, heart rate of 125/min, and pupils that are 8 mm and reactive. Which of the following intoxicants is the most likely cause of this presentation?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify cocaine intoxication by its sympathomimetic effects: hypertension, tachycardia, and mydriasis.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Cocaine intoxication classically presents with agitation, hypertension, tachycardia, and pupillary dilation (mydriasis). It acts as an indirect sympathomimetic, increasing synaptic concentrations of catecholamines.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Alcohol: Causes CNS depression, slurred speech, and ataxia.
(C) Heroin: Causes pupillary constriction (miosis) and respiratory depression.
(D) Marijuana: Causes conjunctival injection and increased appetite, typically without significant pupillary dilation.

metadata.reviewPearl: Identify cocaine intoxication by its sympathomimetic effects: hypertension, tachycardia, and mydriasis.

questions.reviewPearl: Appetite suppression is a common and potentially significant side effect of stimulant medications used for ADHD.
questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy recently diagnosed with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is started on Methylphenidate. His mother is concerned about potential side effects. Which of the following is a common side effect of this medication that can be useful in patients with obesity?
questions.educationalObjective: Appetite suppression is a common and potentially significant side effect of stimulant medications used for ADHD.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Stimulants such as methylphenidate and amphetamines frequently cause appetite suppression. While this is a monitoring concern in growing children, it is noted to be a "useful side effect" for obese patients.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B, C, D): Stimulants typically cause insomnia rather than somnolence, weight loss rather than gain, and do not cause urinary retention.

metadata.reviewPearl: Appetite suppression is a common and potentially significant side effect of stimulant medications used for ADHD.

questions.reviewPearl: Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome can be induced iatrogenically by giving glucose before thiamine to a B1-deficient patient.
questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with a history of severe anorexia nervosa is hospitalized for weight restoration. Shortly after receiving intravenous fluids containing glucose, she develops acute confusion, ataxia, and horizontal nystagmus. Which of the following steps would have prevented this complication?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Decreasing the rate of intravenous fluids

questions.educationalObjective: Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome can be induced iatrogenically by giving glucose before thiamine to a B1-deficient patient.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: In severely malnourished patients, such as those with anorexia nervosa, thiamine stores are often depleted. Administering glucose without thiamine can iatrogenically precipitate Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome by exhausting the remaining thiamine required for glucose metabolism.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Glucose monotherapy: This is exactly what causes the complication.
(C, D): These do not directly address the thiamine-glucose metabolic cofactor requirement.

metadata.reviewPearl: Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome can be induced iatrogenically by giving glucose before thiamine to a B1-deficient patient.

questions.reviewPearl: Substance use disorder is defined by meeting ≥ 2 criteria related to substance use within a 1-year period.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is being evaluated for her patterns of drug use. To meet the DSM-5 criteria for a substance use disorder, she must meet at least two specific signs within which of the following timeframes?
questions.educationalObjective: Substance use disorder is defined by meeting ≥ 2 criteria related to substance use within a 1-year period.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: A diagnosis of substance use disorder requires a pattern of use leading to clinically significant impairment or distress, manifested by at least two symptoms (e.g., tolerance, withdrawal, cravings, inability to cut down) within a 1-year (12-month) period.

metadata.reviewPearl: Substance use disorder is defined by meeting ≥ 2 criteria related to substance use within a 1-year period.

questions.reviewPearl: Delirium tremens typically occurs 24 days after cessation of alcohol use and presents with autonomic instability and altered mental status.
questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a 20-year history of daily heavy alcohol consumption is admitted to the hospital following a motor vehicle accident. Three days after admission, he becomes increasingly confused, agitated, and tremulous. Vital signs are significant for a heart rate of 115/min and blood pressure of 160/95 mmHg. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Delirium tremens typically occurs 24 days after cessation of alcohol use and presents with autonomic instability and altered mental status.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is presenting with Delirium Tremens (DTs), the most severe form of alcohol withdrawal. DTs are characterized by autonomic excitation (tachycardia, hypertension), agitation, tremor, and altered sensorium occurring 4896 hours (24 days) after the last drink.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Acute Stress Disorder: Follows a traumatic event but does not cause autonomic instability or delirium.
(B) Alcoholic Hallucinosis: Occurs earlier (1224 hours) and is characterized by stable vital signs.

metadata.reviewPearl: Delirium tremens typically occurs 24 days after cessation of alcohol use and presents with autonomic instability and altered mental status.

questions.reviewPearl: Bath salt intoxication presents with prolonged psychosis and agitation (up to 1 week) and is often not detected on routine tox screens.
questions.stem: A 26-year-old male is brought to the emergency department after a period of intense combativeness and paranoia. He denies using PCP or cocaine, and his initial urine toxicology screen is negative. Despite inpatient management, he remains psychotic and agitated for one full week before his symptoms subside. Which of the following substances is the most likely cause of this prolonged presentation?
questions.educationalObjective: Bath salt intoxication presents with prolonged psychosis and agitation (up to 1 week) and is often not detected on routine tox screens.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Synthetic cathinones ("bath salts") can cause agitation and psychosis similar to PCP but with a significantly longer duration, sometimes lasting a week. Furthermore, many bath salts are not detected by routine hospital urine toxicology screens.

metadata.reviewPearl: Bath salt intoxication presents with prolonged psychosis and agitation (up to 1 week) and is often not detected on routine tox screens.

questions.reviewPearl: IV benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for managing agitation and sympathomimetic effects in acute cocaine toxicity.
questions.stem: A 28-year-old male presents to the emergency department with severe chest pain and agitation after "partying" with friends. Physical examination reveals dilated pupils and hypertension. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacological intervention for his agitation and cardiovascular symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: IV benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for managing agitation and sympathomimetic effects in acute cocaine toxicity.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: In acute cocaine toxicity (chest pain, agitation, dilated pupils), intravenous benzodiazepines are the treatment of choice to reduce agitation, lower myocardial oxygen demand, and alleviate cardiovascular symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (C) Propranolol: Selective beta-blockers are contraindicated due to the risk of unopposed alpha-adrenergic stimulation.

metadata.reviewPearl: IV benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for managing agitation and sympathomimetic effects in acute cocaine toxicity.

questions.reviewPearl: Benzodiazepines are the treatment of choice for acute alcohol withdrawal.
questions.stem: A 42-year-old male with a history of alcohol use disorder is hospitalized for pneumonia. On his second day of admission, he begins to experience severe tremors and diaphoresis. Which of the following is the preferred pharmacological management for his alcohol withdrawal?
questions.educationalObjective: Benzodiazepines are the treatment of choice for acute alcohol withdrawal.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Benzodiazepines, such as IV lorazepam, are the pharmacological treatment of choice for managing alcohol withdrawal and preventing progression to delirium tremens. They should be titrated until the desired level of sedation is achieved.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B, D): Disulfiram and naltrexone are used to prevent alcohol relapse, not to treat acute withdrawal.

metadata.reviewPearl: Benzodiazepines are the treatment of choice for acute alcohol withdrawal.

questions.reviewPearl: Alcoholic hallucinosis presents within 24 hours of abstinence with stable vitals and orientation, unlike delirium tremens.
questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with visual hallucinations 12 hours after being admitted for a minor injury. He has a known history of heavy alcohol use. On examination, he is alert and oriented, and his vital signs are within normal limits. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Alcoholic hallucinosis presents within 24 hours of abstinence with stable vitals and orientation, unlike delirium tremens.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Alcoholic hallucinosis typically develops within 1224 hours of alcohol cessation. It is distinguished from delirium tremens by the presence of an intact sensorium (the patient is oriented) and stable vital signs.

metadata.reviewPearl: Alcoholic hallucinosis presents within 24 hours of abstinence with stable vitals and orientation, unlike delirium tremens.

questions.reviewPearl: Nasal erythema and weight loss in a young person are common indicators of intranasal cocaine use.
questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman is evaluated for a "clogged nose" and irritability. She mentions she has lost weight recently because she has "lots of energy" and isn't hungry. Physical examination reveals erythema of the nasal turbinates and septum. Her BMI is 19.5 kg/m�. Which of the following conditions is most likely responsible for these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Nasal erythema and weight loss in a young person are common indicators of intranasal cocaine use.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Cocaine use disorder (specifically "snorting" cocaine) classically presents with irritability, weight loss, and erythema or damage to the nasal turbinates and septum.

metadata.reviewPearl: Nasal erythema and weight loss in a young person are common indicators of intranasal cocaine use.

questions.reviewPearl: Recognize methamphetamine abuse by the combination of paranoid psychosis, severe dental decay, and skin excoriations.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old male presents with paranoid delusions and the sensation that "bugs are crawling under his skin." He is notably thin and has poor dentition with multiple excoriated sores on his face. Which of the following intoxicants is most strongly associated with this clinical picture?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize methamphetamine abuse by the combination of paranoid psychosis, severe dental decay, and skin excoriations.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Methamphetamine abuse is associated with agitation, paranoid delusions, and tactile hallucinations (formication, or "bugs under the skin"). Chronic use often leads to "meth mouth" (severe tooth decay) and skin sores from compulsive picking.

metadata.reviewPearl: Recognize methamphetamine abuse by the combination of paranoid psychosis, severe dental decay, and skin excoriations.

questions.reviewPearl: Lorazepam is the preferred benzodiazepine for alcohol withdrawal in patients with hepatic impairment.
questions.stem: A 50-year-old male with chronic alcoholic liver disease is hospitalized for severe alcohol withdrawal. When choosing a benzodiazepine for his management, which of the following is preferred due to its safer metabolism in the setting of hepatic dysfunction?
questions.educationalObjective: Lorazepam is the preferred benzodiazepine for alcohol withdrawal in patients with hepatic impairment.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: While long-acting benzodiazepines like diazepam and chlordiazepoxide are used for withdrawal, lorazepam (along with oxazepam and temazepammnemonic: OTL) is preferred in patients with hepatic disease because it undergoes direct glucuronidation and does not have active metabolites that require oxidative metabolism.

metadata.reviewPearl: Lorazepam is the preferred benzodiazepine for alcohol withdrawal in patients with hepatic impairment.

questions.reviewPearl: A score of ≥ 2 on the CAGE questionnaire indicates a high likelihood of alcohol misuse.
questions.stem: A primary care physician is screening a patient for problematic drinking using the CAGE questionnaire. How many positive responses are required as a "cutoff point" to suggest a potential alcohol use disorder?
questions.educationalObjective: A score of ≥ 2 on the CAGE questionnaire indicates a high likelihood of alcohol misuse.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The CAGE questions (Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener) are a common screening tool for alcohol abuse. A cutoff point of ≥ 2 positive responses is considered suggestive of a problem.

metadata.reviewPearl: A score of ≥ 2 on the CAGE questionnaire indicates a high likelihood of alcohol misuse.

questions.reviewPearl: Understand the clinical definition of tolerance as a requirement for escalating doses to maintain a specific psychoactive effect.
questions.stem: A patient reports that they now need to consume eight drinks per night to achieve the same "buzz" they used to get from four drinks. According to the definitions of substance-related phenomena, which of the following terms describes this need for increased amounts?
questions.educationalObjective: Understand the clinical definition of tolerance as a requirement for escalating doses to maintain a specific psychoactive effect.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Tolerance is defined as the need for increased amounts of a substance to achieve the desired effect, or a diminished effect when using the same amount. Withdrawal is a substance-specific syndrome resulting from the cessation or reduction of prolonged use.

metadata.reviewPearl: Understand the clinical definition of tolerance as a requirement for escalating doses to maintain a specific psychoactive effect.

questions.reviewPearl: Heavy drinking thresholds vary by gender and age, with ≥ 14 drinks/week being the limit for young/middle-aged men.
questions.stem: According to standard definitions for hazardous drinking, what is the threshold for "heavy drinking" in a 40-year-old man?
questions.educationalObjective: Heavy drinking thresholds vary by gender and age, with ≥ 14 drinks/week being the limit for young/middle-aged men.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: In men under age 65, heavy drinking is defined as ≥ 14 drinks per week or ≥ 4 drinks per day. For women and men aged 65+, the threshold is lower at ≥ 7 drinks per week or ≥ 3 per day.

metadata.reviewPearl: Heavy drinking thresholds vary by gender and age, with ≥ 14 drinks/week being the limit for young/middle-aged men.

questions.reviewPearl: Disulfiram is an aversive therapy indicated only for abstinent patients to prevent alcohol relapse.
questions.stem: A 40-year-old male with alcohol use disorder has successfully completed a detoxification program and is now abstinent. He is highly motivated to stay sober but is worried about the "gut-wrenching sickness" he feels if he relapses. Which of the following second-line aversive agents should only be used in patients who are currently abstinent?
questions.educationalObjective: Disulfiram is an aversive therapy indicated only for abstinent patients to prevent alcohol relapse.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Disulfiram is an aversive agent (acetaldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitor) that causes severe illness if alcohol is consumed. It is a second-line agent and should only be used in patients who are currently abstinent, as it is not preferred in patients who are actively drinking.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A, D): Acamprosate and naltrexone are first-line agents for preventing relapse.

metadata.reviewPearl: Disulfiram is an aversive therapy indicated only for abstinent patients to prevent alcohol relapse.
