questions.reviewPearl: Distinguish benzodiazepine overdose from opioid overdose by the lack of pupillary constriction in benzodiazepines.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old male is found unresponsive in an alley. Physical examination reveals a respiratory rate of 8/min and slurred speech. His pupils are 5 mm (normal size). Which of the following overdoses is most consistent with these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Distinguish benzodiazepine overdose from opioid overdose by the lack of pupillary constriction in benzodiazepines.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Benzodiazepine overdose presents with CNS depression (slurred speech, lethargy) and respiratory depression, but with normal pupils. This distinguishes it from opioid overdose (e.g., heroin, methadone), which classically presents with pupillary constriction (miosis).

metadata.reviewPearl: Distinguish benzodiazepine overdose from opioid overdose by the lack of pupillary constriction in benzodiazepines.

questions.reviewPearl: Recognize that alcohol and sedative-hypnotic withdrawal syndromes are medical emergencies due to their potential lethality.
questions.stem: When evaluating patients for drug withdrawal syndromes, cessation of which of the following substances carries the highest risk for life-threatening withdrawal?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize that alcohol and sedative-hypnotic withdrawal syndromes are medical emergencies due to their potential lethality.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Withdrawal from alcohol and sedative-hypnotics (e.g., barbiturates and benzodiazepines) can be life-threatening due to the risk of seizures and autonomic instability. Withdrawal from opiates, cocaine, and cannabis, while distressing, is typically not life-threatening.

metadata.reviewPearl: Recognize that alcohol and sedative-hypnotic withdrawal syndromes are medical emergencies due to their potential lethality.

questions.retrievalTag: Stimulation of serotonin and sympathetic pathways
questions.reviewPearl: MDMA intoxication is characterized by increased sympathomimetic activity and risk of serotonin syndrome.
questions.stem: A 19-year-old college student is brought to the ED after using MDMA (Ecstasy). He is hyperthermic and agitated. Which of the following mechanisms is responsible for the symptoms of MDMA intoxication?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Stimulation of serotonin and sympathetic pathways

questions.educationalObjective: MDMA intoxication is characterized by increased sympathomimetic activity and risk of serotonin syndrome.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: MDMA belongs to the amphetamine class of drugs. It increases sympathetic nervous system activity and can lead to Serotonin Syndrome by increasing synaptic serotonin levels.

metadata.retrievalTag: Stimulation of serotonin and sympathetic pathways
metadata.reviewPearl: MDMA intoxication is characterized by increased sympathomimetic activity and risk of serotonin syndrome.

questions.reviewPearl: Alcoholic cerebellar degeneration involves Purkinje cell loss in the cerebellar vermis, leading to truncal ataxia.
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with decades of heavy alcohol abuse presents with a wide-based gait and truncal ataxia. Physical examination reveals difficulty with rapid alternating movements but intact finger-to-nose testing. Which of the following cells are primarily damaged in the anterior cerebellar vermis in this condition?
questions.educationalObjective: Alcoholic cerebellar degeneration involves Purkinje cell loss in the cerebellar vermis, leading to truncal ataxia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient has alcoholic cerebellar degeneration, caused by damage to the Purkinje cells in the anterior cerebellar vermis. Truncal ataxia (vermis damage) results in postural instability and a wide-based gait, while limb coordination (finger-to-nose) may remain intact.

metadata.reviewPearl: Alcoholic cerebellar degeneration involves Purkinje cell loss in the cerebellar vermis, leading to truncal ataxia.

questions.reviewPearl: Be aware that dextromethorphan can trigger a false positive for PCP on a routine toxicology screen.
questions.stem: A patient is undergoing a routine urine drug screen for work. They recently took Dextromethorphan for a severe cough. Which of the following substances may return as a falsely positive result on their drug screen?
questions.educationalObjective: Be aware that dextromethorphan can trigger a false positive for PCP on a routine toxicology screen.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Commonly used medications like Dextromethorphan can cause a falsely positive result for PCP on a standard urine drug screen.

metadata.reviewPearl: Be aware that dextromethorphan can trigger a false positive for PCP on a routine toxicology screen.

questions.reviewPearl: The combination of violent behavior, rotatory nystagmus, and analgesia is highly suggestive of PCP intoxication.
questions.stem: A 22-year-old male is brought to the ED by police after being found acting violently. On examination, he has rotatory nystagmus, hypertonia, and a decreased sensation to pinprick. What is the likely intoxicant?
questions.educationalObjective: The combination of violent behavior, rotatory nystagmus, and analgesia is highly suggestive of PCP intoxication.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: PCP (phencyclidine) intoxication is characterized by severe agitation, violent behavior, nystagmus, hypertonia, and a characteristic insensitivity to pain (decreased sensation to pinprick).

metadata.reviewPearl: The combination of violent behavior, rotatory nystagmus, and analgesia is highly suggestive of PCP intoxication.

questions.reviewPearl: Perioral or perinasal dermatitis in an adolescent with acute neurological symptoms is a hallmark of inhalant abuse.
questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy is found drowsy and complaining of a headache. His mother notes a mild rash around his mouth that was not there earlier. His toxicology screen is negative. This presentation is classic for the abuse of which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Perioral or perinasal dermatitis in an adolescent with acute neurological symptoms is a hallmark of inhalant abuse.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Inhalant abuse (e.g., glue, toluene) is common in adolescents and can cause transient CNS depression. A perioral "glue sniffer's rash" is a classic physical exam finding due to chemical exposure.

metadata.reviewPearl: Perioral or perinasal dermatitis in an adolescent with acute neurological symptoms is a hallmark of inhalant abuse.

questions.reviewPearl: Caffeine intoxication manifests as overstimulation of the sympathetic nervous system.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old male presents with anxiety, jitteriness, and heart palpitations. He mentions he has been consuming multiple "energy drinks" daily to stay awake for his night shift. These symptoms are consistent with which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Caffeine intoxication manifests as overstimulation of the sympathetic nervous system.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Excessive consumption of caffeine (e.g., from energy drinks) can cause caffeine intoxication, presenting with sympathetic stimulation signs such as anxiety, jitteriness, insomnia, palpitations, and tremor.

metadata.reviewPearl: Caffeine intoxication manifests as overstimulation of the sympathetic nervous system.

questions.reviewPearl: Headache is the most common and classic feature of caffeine withdrawal.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman decides to "quit coffee" abruptly. Three days later, she presents to her primary care physician with a severe, throbbing headache. What is the most likely cause?
questions.educationalObjective: Headache is the most common and classic feature of caffeine withdrawal.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The classic symptom of caffeine withdrawal is a headache, which occurs when a regular user abruptly stops their intake.

metadata.reviewPearl: Headache is the most common and classic feature of caffeine withdrawal.

questions.reviewPearl: The success of interventions for substance misuse often depends on the patient's motivational stage and readiness to change.
questions.stem: A physician is conducting a brief intervention for a patient with risky drinking habits. Which of the following factors is the most important determinant of the effectiveness of this intervention?
questions.educationalObjective: The success of interventions for substance misuse often depends on the patient's motivational stage and readiness to change.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Effective brief interventions for alcohol abuse require an understanding of the patient's level of awareness of the problem and their readiness to change.

metadata.reviewPearl: The success of interventions for substance misuse often depends on the patient's motivational stage and readiness to change.

questions.reviewPearl: Behavioral counseling is the first-line intervention for patients identified as hazardous drinkers through screening.
questions.stem: Which type of intervention is the recommended primary strategy for adults who engage in risky or hazardous drinking but do not yet meet the full criteria for a substance use disorder?
questions.educationalObjective: Behavioral counseling is the first-line intervention for patients identified as hazardous drinkers through screening.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: For adults (including pregnant women) who engage in risky or hazardous drinking, the primary recommended intervention is behavioral counseling.

metadata.reviewPearl: Behavioral counseling is the first-line intervention for patients identified as hazardous drinkers through screening.

questions.retrievalTag: It is not proven to be more effective
questions.reviewPearl: Dronabinol is generally ineffective for the weight gain goals of cancer cachexia.
questions.stem: A 65-year-old male with terminal pancreatic cancer has severe anorexia and weight loss (cachexia). His family asks about starting Dronabinol to help improve his appetite and weight. What should the physician advise regarding its efficacy in this specific setting?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: It is the first-line treatment for cancer cachexia

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: It is not proven to be more effective than placebo

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: It should only be used if the patient also has nausea

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: It is only effective in children

questions.educationalObjective: Dronabinol is generally ineffective for the weight gain goals of cancer cachexia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Synthetic cannabinoids such as dronabinol have not been proven to be more effective than placebo for treating cancer-related anorexia/cachexia syndrome (CACS). It may, however, be used as second-line therapy for chemotherapy-associated nausea and vomiting.

metadata.retrievalTag: It is not proven to be more effective
metadata.reviewPearl: Dronabinol is generally ineffective for the weight gain goals of cancer cachexia.

questions.reviewPearl: Alcoholic neuropathy presents as a symmetric, distal burning pain.
questions.stem: A 52-year-old male with chronic alcoholism presents with a symmetric distal polyneuropathy. He describes the pain in his feet as a "burning" sensation. What is the likely cause?
questions.educationalObjective: Alcoholic neuropathy presents as a symmetric, distal burning pain.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Alcoholic neuropathy is a toxic neuropathy that typically results in a symmetric distal polyneuropathy, with a characteristic burning type pain in the extremities.

metadata.reviewPearl: Alcoholic neuropathy presents as a symmetric, distal burning pain.

questions.retrievalTag: Detecting subtle changes in mental status
questions.reviewPearl: The Reitan trail test is a sensitive tool for identifying early or minor cognitive fluctuations.
questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with mild cognitive decline is given a timed connect-the-numbers test during his evaluation. Which of the following best describes the indication for this test?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Detecting subtle changes in mental status

questions.educationalObjective: The Reitan trail test is a sensitive tool for identifying early or minor cognitive fluctuations.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The Reitan trail test (connect-the-numbers) is a timed test that is particularly useful for detecting subtle mental status changes.

metadata.retrievalTag: Detecting subtle changes in mental status
metadata.reviewPearl: The Reitan trail test is a sensitive tool for identifying early or minor cognitive fluctuations.

questions.retrievalTag: It is highly sensitive but not specific
questions.reviewPearl: The MMSE is a sensitive screening tool, meaning it is good for ruling out dementia when scores are normal.
questions.stem: When evaluating a screening tool for dementia, such as the Mini-Mental State Exam (MMSE), which of the following statements is true regarding its diagnostic utility?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: It is highly specific but not sensitive

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: It is highly sensitive but not specific

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: It is both highly sensitive and highly specific

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: It is neither sensitive nor specific

questions.educationalObjective: The MMSE is a sensitive screening tool, meaning it is good for ruling out dementia when scores are normal.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The MMSE is sensitive but not specific (mnemonic: Sn-N-OUT). If a highly sensitive test is negative, you can rule out the disease, but a positive result does not confirm it because of its lower specificity.

metadata.retrievalTag: It is highly sensitive but not specific
metadata.reviewPearl: The MMSE is a sensitive screening tool, meaning it is good for ruling out dementia when scores are normal.

questions.reviewPearl: Physicians determine clinical capacity; judges determine legal competence.
questions.stem: A 78-year-old patient with early dementia is being evaluated by her physician for her ability to consent to a procedure. Which of the following terms correctly describes the legal determination of her status, which must be decided by a judge?
questions.educationalObjective: Physicians determine clinical capacity; judges determine legal competence.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Competence is a legal term and can be decided only by a judge, whereas capacity is a clinical term and may be assessed by physicians.

metadata.reviewPearl: Physicians determine clinical capacity; judges determine legal competence.

questions.reviewPearl: The Superego is the aspect of the psyche that internalizes societal and moral standards.
questions.stem: According to Freud's structural model of the psyche, which component represents the "moral conscience" and the idealized inner image of oneself influenced by parental and societal teachings?
questions.educationalObjective: The Superego is the aspect of the psyche that internalizes societal and moral standards.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The Superego represents morality, society, and parental teaching. It acts as a moral conscience and the "ego ideal". The Id represents unconscious instinctual urges, and the Ego serves as the mediator.

metadata.reviewPearl: The Superego is the aspect of the psyche that internalizes societal and moral standards.

questions.reviewPearl: Controlling involves managing external factors to gain a sense of security against anxiety.
questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman feels significant anxiety regarding her upcoming travel. To cope, she spends hours every night meticulously regulating the itinerary, hotel check-in times, and backup transportation options. Which immature defense mechanism involves regulating situations or events in the external environment to relieve anxiety?
questions.educationalObjective: Controlling involves managing external factors to gain a sense of security against anxiety.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Controlling is an immature defense mechanism defined as regulating situations or events of the external environment to relieve anxiety.

metadata.reviewPearl: Controlling involves managing external factors to gain a sense of security against anxiety.

questions.reviewPearl: Undoing is an action performed to symbolically reverse an unacceptable thought or impulse.
questions.stem: A man who has had a brief, intrusive fantasy of "killing his wife" by sabotaging her car immediately decides to take the car to a mechanic for a complete, expensive safety checkup. Which immature defense mechanism is he utilizing?
questions.educationalObjective: Undoing is an action performed to symbolically reverse an unacceptable thought or impulse.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Undoing is an immature defense mechanism defined as attempting to reverse a situation or thought by adopting a new, compensatory behavior.

metadata.reviewPearl: Undoing is an action performed to symbolically reverse an unacceptable thought or impulse.

questions.reviewPearl: Psychoanalysis is an intensive, insight-oriented therapy targeting unconscious conflicts.
questions.stem: A patient is referred for a type of psychotherapy that focuses on resolving unconscious conflicts by bringing repressed feelings into awareness. The therapist and patient meet 35 days per week for many years. What is this type of psychotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Psychoanalysis is an intensive, insight-oriented therapy targeting unconscious conflicts.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Psychoanalysis focuses on resolving unconscious conflicts by bringing repressed experiences and feelings into awareness (insight-oriented). It is intensive, typically requiring sessions 35 days per week for many years.

metadata.reviewPearl: Psychoanalysis is an intensive, insight-oriented therapy targeting unconscious conflicts.

questions.reviewPearl: Biofeedback teaches patients to voluntarily control autonomic responses to treat various clinical conditions.
questions.stem: A patient with migraines is given real-time heart rate and blood pressure measurements while being instructed to mentally control the visceral changes that affect her pain. This type of behavioral therapy is known as:
questions.educationalObjective: Biofeedback teaches patients to voluntarily control autonomic responses to treat various clinical conditions.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Biofeedback involves using signals from the body (e.g., heart rate, muscle tension, blood pressure) as indicators of emotional distress to help patients gain control over physiological reactions. It can be used for agoraphobia, migraines, and hypertension.

metadata.reviewPearl: Biofeedback teaches patients to voluntarily control autonomic responses to treat various clinical conditions.

questions.reviewPearl: Cognitive therapy identifies and modifies cognitive distortions to improve psychiatric symptoms.
questions.stem: Which specific type of psychotherapy seeks to correct faulty assumptions and negative thought patterns (e.g., overgeneralization or catastrophizing) that exacerbate psychiatric symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Cognitive therapy identifies and modifies cognitive distortions to improve psychiatric symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Cognitive therapy (often part of CBT) seeks to correct faulty assumptions and negative feelings by teaching the patient to identify and replace maladaptive thoughts.

metadata.reviewPearl: Cognitive therapy identifies and modifies cognitive distortions to improve psychiatric symptoms.

questions.reviewPearl: EMDR is a specialized therapy specifically indicated for the management of PTSD.
questions.stem: A 29-year-old veteran is being evaluated for nightmares and avoidance behaviors. Which complex method of psychotherapy, which integrates exposure with specific eye movements, is helpful for this patient's condition?
questions.educationalObjective: EMDR is a specialized therapy specifically indicated for the management of PTSD.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR) is a complex psychotherapy method helpful for patients with Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD).

metadata.reviewPearl: EMDR is a specialized therapy specifically indicated for the management of PTSD.

questions.reviewPearl: Supportive psychotherapy focuses on crisis management and strengthening current coping mechanisms.
questions.stem: A therapist is working with a low-functioning patient who is currently in a state of crisis. The therapist focuses on reinforcing the patient's ability to cope with immediate stressors rather than gaining insight into unconscious conflicts. This type of therapy is known as:
questions.educationalObjective: Supportive psychotherapy focuses on crisis management and strengthening current coping mechanisms.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Supportive psychotherapy is used to reinforce a patient's ability to cope with stressors and is commonly used for low-functioning patients or those in crisis who are at risk of decompensation.

metadata.reviewPearl: Supportive psychotherapy focuses on crisis management and strengthening current coping mechanisms.

questions.reviewPearl: Distinguish IADLs (complex living skills) from ADLs (basic self-care) in functional assessments.
questions.stem: When evaluating a geriatric patient's ability to live independently, a social worker assesses the patient's ability to manage finances and go shopping. These activities are categorized as which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Distinguish IADLs (complex living skills) from ADLs (basic self-care) in functional assessments.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADLs) are more advanced tasks required for independent living, such as managing finances, shopping, or cooking. ADLs are basic self-care tasks like eating, bathing, and dressing.

metadata.reviewPearl: Distinguish IADLs (complex living skills) from ADLs (basic self-care) in functional assessments.

questions.reviewPearl: Certain neurological changes, such as limited upward gaze and absent DTRs, can be benign consequences of normal aging.
questions.stem: Which of the following is considered a normal physical examination finding in a healthy 85-year-old patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Certain neurological changes, such as limited upward gaze and absent DTRs, can be benign consequences of normal aging.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: A decreased upward gaze is a normal finding in elderly patients. Other normal findings include the absence of deep tendon reflexes (DTRs).

metadata.reviewPearl: Certain neurological changes, such as limited upward gaze and absent DTRs, can be benign consequences of normal aging.

questions.retrievalTag: It is an abnormal finding that warrants further
questions.reviewPearl: Rotatory nystagmus in an elderly patient is a pathological finding requiring diagnostic workup.
questions.stem: During the physical examination of a 78-year-old man, the physician notes brisk rotatory nystagmus when the patient looks to the side. What is the most appropriate interpretation of this finding?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: It is a normal part of the aging process

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: It is characteristic of early Alzheimer's

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: It is a normal finding if the patient has BPPV

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: It is an abnormal finding that warrants further evaluation

questions.educationalObjective: Rotatory nystagmus in an elderly patient is a pathological finding requiring diagnostic workup.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Brisk rotatory nystagmus on lateral gaze is not a normal finding in elderly patients and warrants further medical evaluation. In contrast, horizontal nystagmus can be normal in patients with BPPV.

metadata.retrievalTag: It is an abnormal finding that warrants further
metadata.reviewPearl: Rotatory nystagmus in an elderly patient is a pathological finding requiring diagnostic workup.

questions.retrievalTag: Being unable to remember specific instances of forgetfulness
questions.reviewPearl: Lack of awareness or inability to recall episodes of forgetfulness is a red flag for dementia.
questions.stem: When evaluating memory in the elderly, which of the following is a sign of pathological decline rather than normal aging?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Forgetting the name of a distant acquaintance

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Occasional forgetfulness of where items were placed

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Being unable to remember specific instances of forgetfulness

questions.educationalObjective: Lack of awareness or inability to recall episodes of forgetfulness is a red flag for dementia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: It is not normal for elderly patients to be unable to remember specific instances of forgetfulness; this suggests a pathological cognitive disorder. Patients with normal aging are often aware of and concerned about their memory loss.

metadata.retrievalTag: Being unable to remember specific instances of forgetfulness
metadata.reviewPearl: Lack of awareness or inability to recall episodes of forgetfulness is a red flag for dementia.

questions.retrievalTag: Inability to recall important recent events and conversations
questions.reviewPearl: Loss of recall for recent significant information is a hallmark of pathological cognitive decline.
questions.stem: A daughter is concerned that her 82-year-old mother is becoming increasingly forgetful. Which of the following observations most clearly indicates dementia rather than normal age-related changes?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Forgetting where she put her reading glasses

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Inability to recall important recent events and conversations

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Taking longer to learn how to use a new smartphone

questions.educationalObjective: Loss of recall for recent significant information is a hallmark of pathological cognitive decline.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: An inability to recall important recent events and conversations is a sign of dementia, not normal aging. Other abnormal signs include getting lost in familiar territory or being unable to operate common appliances.

metadata.retrievalTag: Inability to recall important recent events and conversations
metadata.reviewPearl: Loss of recall for recent significant information is a hallmark of pathological cognitive decline.

questions.reviewPearl: Physical signs of trauma in the genital or perineal region in vulnerable elderly patients must be evaluated for sexual abuse.
questions.stem: An 80-year-old female resident of a nursing home is brought to the clinic. Physical examination reveals a friable perineal laceration and postmenopausal bleeding. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of these findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Physical signs of trauma in the genital or perineal region in vulnerable elderly patients must be evaluated for sexual abuse.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: In an 80-year-old nursing home resident, findings of friable perineal lacerations and postmenopausal bleeding are highly suggestive of elder sexual abuse.

metadata.reviewPearl: Physical signs of trauma in the genital or perineal region in vulnerable elderly patients must be evaluated for sexual abuse.

questions.reviewPearl: The diagnosis of PMS requires a cluster of ≥ 5 cyclic symptoms.
questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman reports intense irritability, mood swings, and gastrointestinal upset that occur exactly one week before her menses. According to diagnostic criteria for Premenstrual Syndrome (PMS), what is the minimum number of physical and/or behavioral features required for diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: The diagnosis of PMS requires a cluster of ≥ 5 cyclic symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: To be diagnosed with premenstrual syndrome, patients require > 5 physical and/or behavioral clinical features.

metadata.reviewPearl: The diagnosis of PMS requires a cluster of ≥ 5 cyclic symptoms.

questions.reviewPearl: SSRIs are the first-line pharmacotherapy for the management of severe premenstrual dysphoric symptoms.
questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is diagnosed with Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder (PMDD), characterized by severe agitation and sadness that cause disturbances in her daily functional capacity. Which class of medication is the preferred treatment for this condition?
questions.educationalObjective: SSRIs are the first-line pharmacotherapy for the management of severe premenstrual dysphoric symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: SSRIs may be used to treat premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), which is considered a severe form of PMS.

metadata.reviewPearl: SSRIs are the first-line pharmacotherapy for the management of severe premenstrual dysphoric symptoms.

questions.reviewPearl: Fluphenazine and other typical antipsychotics can disrupt hypothalamic thermoregulation, increasing the risk of hypothermia in patients with environmental exposure risks (e.g., homeless populations).
questions.stem: A 48-year-old homeless male with a history of schizophrenia is brought to the emergency department during the winter months after being found unresponsive on a park bench. His core body temperature is 89.6�F (32.0�C). His medical records indicate he receives monthly depot injections of an antipsychotic medication. Which of the following medications is most likely responsible for increasing this patient's susceptibility to environmental temperature changes?
questions.educationalObjective: Fluphenazine and other typical antipsychotics can disrupt hypothalamic thermoregulation, increasing the risk of hypothermia in patients with environmental exposure risks (e.g., homeless populations).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Typical antipsychotics, particularly high-potency agents like fluphenazine, can occasionally cause hypothermia by disrupting thermoregulation. They affect the hypothalamus, leading to an improper physiological response to heat and cold (such as disrupting the body's shivering mechanism).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A, D) Aripiprazole and Quetiapine: These are atypical antipsychotics and are less classically associated with severe thermoregulatory disruption compared to typical antipsychotics like fluphenazine.
(B) Clozapine: While an atypical antipsychotic, its major life-threatening side effect is agranulocytosis, not primary hypothalamic thermoregulatory disruption.

metadata.reviewPearl: Fluphenazine and other typical antipsychotics can disrupt hypothalamic thermoregulation, increasing the risk of hypothermia in patients with environmental exposure risks (e.g., homeless populations).

questions.reviewPearl: Clozapine is the antipsychotic with the lowest risk of causing tardive dyskinesia and is the preferred replacement agent when severe extrapyramidal side effects or TD emerge.
questions.stem: A 34-year-old male with a long-standing history of schizophrenia presents to the clinic for a follow-up. He has been taking a second-generation antipsychotic for the past three years. During the interview, he frequently smacks his lips, protrudes his tongue, and exhibits choreoathetoid movements of his fingers. The physician determines that an antipsychotic dose reduction is not possible due to a high risk of psychosis relapse. Which of the following medications should this patient be switched to?
questions.educationalObjective: Clozapine is the antipsychotic with the lowest risk of causing tardive dyskinesia and is the preferred replacement agent when severe extrapyramidal side effects or TD emerge.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This patient is exhibiting symptoms of tardive dyskinesia (TD). Among the atypical (second-generation) antipsychotics, risperidone is the most likely to cause TD, while clozapine is the least likely. If TD develops and dose reduction is not feasible, the standard management is to switch the patient to clozapine.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Haloperidol: A high-potency first-generation antipsychotic that carries a very high risk of extrapyramidal symptoms and would worsen tardive dyskinesia.
(C) Olanzapine: While it has a lower risk of TD than first-generation antipsychotics, clozapine remains the most effective and lowest-risk option for existing TD.
(D) Risperidone: This is the most likely atypical antipsychotic to cause tardive dyskinesia and should be discontinued in this patient.

metadata.reviewPearl: Clozapine is the antipsychotic with the lowest risk of causing tardive dyskinesia and is the preferred replacement agent when severe extrapyramidal side effects or TD emerge.

questions.reviewPearl: Williams syndrome is characterized by "elfin" facies, intellectual disability with strong verbal skills, extreme friendliness toward strangers, and hypercalcemia.
questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl is evaluated for developmental delays. On examination, she is noted to have a broad forehead, a short nose with a broad tip, full cheeks, and a wide mouth with full lips. She is highly verbal, extremely sociable, and inappropriately friendly with the clinical staff she has just met. Laboratory testing reveals an elevated serum calcium level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Williams syndrome is characterized by "elfin" facies, intellectual disability with strong verbal skills, extreme friendliness toward strangers, and hypercalcemia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This patient presents with classic features of Williams syndrome, a microdeletion syndrome. Key findings include an "elfin" facies (broad forehead, short nose, full lips), intellectual disability with well-developed verbal skills, and extreme friendliness with strangers. Patients also frequently present with cardiovascular issues and hypercalcemia due to increased sensitivity to vitamin D.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Down syndrome: Presents with flat facies, prominent epicanthal folds, and a single palmar crease, rather than elfin facies and extreme sociability.
(B) Fetal alcohol syndrome: Characterized by a smooth philtrum, thin vermilion border, and small palpebral fissures, lacking the extreme verbal sociability of Williams syndrome.

(C) Fragile X syndrome: Presents with a long face, large prominent ears, and macroorchidism, and is commonly associated with social anxiety or autism-spectrum behaviors rather than extreme friendliness.

metadata.reviewPearl: Williams syndrome is characterized by "elfin" facies, intellectual disability with strong verbal skills, extreme friendliness toward strangers, and hypercalcemia.

questions.reviewPearl: Ethanol metabolism increases the NADH/NAD+ ratio, which inhibits gluconeogenesis and leads to fasting hypoglycemia, especially when hepatic glycogen stores are depleted.
questions.stem: A 45-year-old male with a 20-year history of heavy alcohol consumption is brought to the emergency department after being found unresponsive following a marathon race. He had not eaten anything prior to the run. His blood glucose is 42 mg/dL. Which of the following metabolic derangements is the primary cause of his hypoglycemia?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Decreased glycogenolysis due to hepatic cirrhosis

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Hyperinsulinemia secondary to pancreatic islet cell hyperplasia

questions.educationalObjective: Ethanol metabolism increases the NADH/NAD+ ratio, which inhibits gluconeogenesis and leads to fasting hypoglycemia, especially when hepatic glycogen stores are depleted.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Alcoholics commonly experience fasting hypoglycemia due to a high NADH/NAD+ ratio. Ethanol metabolism relies on alcohol dehydrogenase and aldehyde dehydrogenase, both of which reduce NAD+ to NADH. The resulting abundance of NADH forces pyruvate to convert to lactate and oxaloacetate to convert to malate, effectively depleting the substrates required for gluconeogenesis. This is especially dangerous when glycogen stores are depleted (e.g., fasting or after exercise).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Decreased glycogenolysis: While liver disease can impair overall metabolic function, the acute mechanism of alcohol-induced fasting hypoglycemia is specifically the failure of gluconeogenesis.
(C) Hyperinsulinemia: Alcohol-induced hypoglycemia is not mediated by insulin excess.
(D) Increased renal excretion: This mechanism is seen with SGLT2 inhibitors or uncontrolled diabetes, not alcohol intoxication.

metadata.reviewPearl: Ethanol metabolism increases the NADH/NAD+ ratio, which inhibits gluconeogenesis and leads to fasting hypoglycemia, especially when hepatic glycogen stores are depleted.

questions.retrievalTag: Jugular venous distention and systemic hypertension
questions.reviewPearl: Obstructive sleep apnea is associated with obesity, daytime sleepiness, and morning headaches. Chronic apneic episodes lead to both systemic and pulmonary hypertension.
questions.stem: A 54-year-old obese man presents to the clinic with complaints of morning headaches, poor concentration, and falling asleep during meetings at work. His wife reports that he snores very loudly and occasionally stops breathing for brief periods during the night. Which of the following physical examination findings is most likely to be present in this patient?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Jugular venous distention and systemic hypertension

questions.educationalObjective: Obstructive sleep apnea is associated with obesity, daytime sleepiness, and morning headaches. Chronic apneic episodes lead to both systemic and pulmonary hypertension.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This patient has classic symptoms of Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), which include loud snoring, apneic episodes, daytime sleepiness, and morning headaches. OSA is strongly associated with obesity and leads to chronic hypoxemia and hypercapnia during sleep. This causes pulmonary vasoconstriction (leading to pulmonary hypertension and right heart failure, hence jugular venous distention) as well as systemic hypertension.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A, C, D): These findings are not associated with the pathophysiology of obstructive sleep apnea.

metadata.retrievalTag: Jugular venous distention and systemic hypertension
metadata.reviewPearl: Obstructive sleep apnea is associated with obesity, daytime sleepiness, and morning headaches. Chronic apneic episodes lead to both systemic and pulmonary hypertension.

questions.reviewPearl: Hypercalcemia classically presents with the tetrad of "stones, bones, groans, and psychiatric overtones," which includes mood disturbances and altered mental status.
questions.stem: A 42-year-old female presents to the emergency department with severe, colicky flank pain, nausea, and diffuse bone pain. Her husband notes that over the past two months she has become increasingly anxious and frequently confused. A urinalysis reveals calcium oxalate crystals. This patient's psychiatric symptoms are most likely directly related to which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Hypercalcemia classically presents with the tetrad of "stones, bones, groans, and psychiatric overtones," which includes mood disturbances and altered mental status.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This patient's presentation of renal stones (flank pain/crystals), bone pain, abdominal pain (nausea), and anxiety/confusion is classic for hypercalcemia ("stones, bones, groans, and psychiatric overtones"). The elevated serum calcium directly alters neuronal membrane permeability, leading to altered mental status, anxiety, and depression.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Hypokalemia: Presents with muscle weakness, cramps, and U waves on EKG, not the classic tetrad described.
(C) Hyponatremia: Can cause confusion and seizures but is not associated with bone pain or renal stones.
(D) Uremia: Causes encephalopathy, but does not specifically cause the tetrad of symptoms seen in hypercalcemia.

metadata.reviewPearl: Hypercalcemia classically presents with the tetrad of "stones, bones, groans, and psychiatric overtones," which includes mood disturbances and altered mental status.

questions.reviewPearl: Maple syrup urine disease presents with sweet-smelling urine, vomiting, and lethargy due to a defect in the metabolism of branched-chain amino acids.
questions.stem: A 3-day-old infant is brought to the pediatric emergency department due to vomiting, lethargy, and poor feeding. The mother mentions that the baby's diapers have an unusual odor that smells like "burnt sugar." This condition is caused by a defect in the degradation of which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Maple syrup urine disease presents with sweet-smelling urine, vomiting, and lethargy due to a defect in the metabolism of branched-chain amino acids.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The infant is presenting with Maple Syrup Urine Disease (MSUD), indicated by poor feeding, vomiting, and urine that smells like burnt sugar or maple syrup. MSUD is caused by a defect in branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase, leading to an inability to degrade branched-chain amino acids (isoleucine, leucine, and valine).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Aromatic amino acids: Defects here cause conditions like PKU (phenylalanine), which presents with a "musty" body odor, not a burnt sugar smell.
(C) Long-chain fatty acids: Defects cause fatty acid oxidation disorders, presenting with hypoketotic hypoglycemia.
(D) Pyrimidine bases: Defects here are not associated with MSUD.

metadata.reviewPearl: Maple syrup urine disease presents with sweet-smelling urine, vomiting, and lethargy due to a defect in the metabolism of branched-chain amino acids.

questions.reviewPearl: Wernicke encephalopathy requires immediate administration of thiamine to correct the underlying Vitamin B1 deficiency and prevent permanent neurological damage.
questions.stem: A 55-year-old male with a long history of alcohol abuse is brought to the emergency department. He is profoundly confused, unable to walk without assistance due to a wide-based gait, and physical examination reveals bilateral nystagmus and an inability to adduct either eye. Administration of which of the following is most critical to address the underlying cause of his symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Wernicke encephalopathy requires immediate administration of thiamine to correct the underlying Vitamin B1 deficiency and prevent permanent neurological damage.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is presenting with the classic triad of Wernicke encephalopathy: confusion, ataxia, and nystagmus/ophthalmoplegia (mnemonic: CAN). This condition is caused by a deficiency in thiamine (Vitamin B1). To prevent progression to irreversible Korsakoff syndrome, thiamine must be administered immediately.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Haloperidol: Used for agitation or psychosis, but does not treat the underlying nutritional deficiency of Wernicke encephalopathy.
(B) Intravenous fluids with dextrose: Administering glucose before thiamine in a malnourished patient will acutely worsen Wernicke encephalopathy by rapidly depleting remaining thiamine stores.
(C) Lorazepam: Used for alcohol withdrawal, which presents with autonomic hyperactivity and tremors, not the Wernicke triad.

metadata.reviewPearl: Wernicke encephalopathy requires immediate administration of thiamine to correct the underlying Vitamin B1 deficiency and prevent permanent neurological damage.

questions.reviewPearl: Cocaine toxicity can cause acute myocardial ischemia by inducing severe coronary artery vasospasm.
questions.stem: A 26-year-old male is rushed to the emergency department after collapsing at a party. He complains of severe, crushing substernal chest pain. His pupils are dilated, and his blood pressure is 180/110 mmHg. An EKG reveals transient ST-segment elevations in the anterior leads. Which of the following is the most likely pathophysiologic mechanism for his cardiac findings?
questions.educationalObjective: Cocaine toxicity can cause acute myocardial ischemia by inducing severe coronary artery vasospasm.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient's presentation of severe chest pain, hypertension, and mydriasis following a party is highly suspicious for cocaine intoxication. Cocaine acts as an indirect sympathomimetic, and its use can induce intense coronary vasospasm, leading to myocardial ischemia and transient ST-segment elevations (Prinzmetal angina).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A, D) Atherosclerosis / Plaque rupture: While cocaine accelerates atherosclerosis over time, the acute, transient ST-elevations in a young, healthy patient without a history of CAD are primarily due to vasospasm.
(C) Myocardial inflammation: This describes myocarditis, which does not typically present with the acute sympathomimetic toxidrome (mydriasis, severe hypertension) seen here.

metadata.reviewPearl: Cocaine toxicity can cause acute myocardial ischemia by inducing severe coronary artery vasospasm.

questions.reviewPearl: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia presents as impaired adduction of the eye ipsilateral to a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF).
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a history of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis presents with a new visual complaint. When asked to look to the left, her right eye remains midline, while her left eye exhibits horizontal nystagmus. Convergence is fully intact. Which of the following structures is most likely damaged in this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia presents as impaired adduction of the eye ipsilateral to a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is exhibiting an internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO), which is an inability to adduct the ipsilateral eye during lateral gaze, accompanied by nystagmus in the contralateral abducting eye. The lesion is located in the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) on the side of the eye that cannot adduct. Because her right eye cannot adduct, she has a right-sided MLF lesion.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Left MLF: A left MLF lesion would cause an inability to adduct the left eye when looking to the right.
(B, D) PPRF: Lesions here cause an ipsilateral horizontal gaze palsy (neither eye can look toward the side of the lesion), rather than a dissociated adduction deficit.

metadata.reviewPearl: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia presents as impaired adduction of the eye ipsilateral to a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF).

questions.reviewPearl: Alzheimer disease is characterized neuroanatomically by widespread cortical atrophy.
questions.stem: A 72-year-old man is brought to the neurologist by his family due to a gradual, progressive decline in memory over the last four years. He frequently forgets the names of his grandchildren and gets lost while walking in his own neighborhood. An MRI of the brain is performed. Which of the following neuroimaging findings is most expected in this patient?

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Atrophy isolated to the frontal and temporal lobes

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Enlargement of the ventricles with normal sulci

questions.educationalObjective: Alzheimer disease is characterized neuroanatomically by widespread cortical atrophy.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: This patient's clinical presentation is classic for Alzheimer disease, the most common cause of dementia in the elderly. It is characterized by an insidious onset of memory impairment. Neuroimaging classically reveals widespread cortical atrophy, particularly prominent in the medial temporal lobes and hippocampus initially, before progressing globally.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Atrophy isolated to the frontal and temporal lobes: This is characteristic of Frontotemporal dementia (Pick disease), which typically presents earlier with prominent personality and behavioral changes rather than primary memory loss.
(C) Multiple small subcortical infarcts: This is the hallmark of Vascular dementia, which usually presents with a step-wise decline in cognition.
(D) Enlargement of the ventricles with normal sulci: This describes Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH), which presents with the triad of magnetic gait, urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline.

metadata.reviewPearl: Alzheimer disease is characterized neuroanatomically by widespread cortical atrophy.

questions.reviewPearl: Vascular dementia is characterized by a step-wise decline in cognition in patients with established cardiovascular risk factors.
questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of poorly controlled hypertension and atherosclerosis is evaluated for cognitive decline. His family notes that his memory and daily functioning have worsened in distinct "drops" every few months, rather than a smooth, gradual decline. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Vascular dementia is characterized by a step-wise decline in cognition in patients with established cardiovascular risk factors.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Vascular dementia is caused by multiple arterial infarcts or chronic ischemia. It classically presents in patients with significant vascular risk factors (e.g., hypertension, atherosclerosis) and follows a step-wise decline in cognitive function, directly correlating with distinct ischemic events.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Alzheimer disease: Presents with a smooth, continuous, and insidious decline in memory.
(B) Lewy body dementia: Characterized by fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism.
(C) Pick disease: Frontotemporal dementia presents with early personality/behavioral changes and a smooth decline.

metadata.reviewPearl: Vascular dementia is characterized by a step-wise decline in cognition in patients with established cardiovascular risk factors.

questions.reviewPearl: Frontotemporal dementia (Pick disease) presents with early personality and behavioral changes due to localized degeneration of the frontal and temporal cortices.
questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman is brought to the clinic by her husband because of a drastic change in her personality over the last year. Previously quiet and polite, she now frequently makes inappropriate sexual remarks to strangers and shows no empathy or concern when her husband is upset. Her memory remains largely intact. An MRI of the brain is most likely to show atrophy in which of the following regions?
questions.educationalObjective: Frontotemporal dementia (Pick disease) presents with early personality and behavioral changes due to localized degeneration of the frontal and temporal cortices.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is presenting with Frontotemporal dementia (Pick disease). This degenerative condition preferentially affects the frontal and temporal lobes, sparing the parietal and occipital lobes. Clinically, it is characterized by early and prominent changes in personality, behavior (disinhibition, hypersexuality), and language, often with relative preservation of memory until later stages.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B, C) Occipital and Parietal cortex: These regions are typically spared in frontotemporal dementia.
(D) Widespread diffuse cortical areas: This is characteristic of Alzheimer disease, which generally presents with early prominent memory loss rather than disinhibition.

metadata.reviewPearl: Frontotemporal dementia (Pick disease) presents with early personality and behavioral changes due to localized degeneration of the frontal and temporal cortices.

questions.reviewPearl: MPTP is a neurotoxin that destroys the substantia nigra, leading to an acute onset of drug-induced Parkinson disease.
questions.stem: A 24-year-old male with a history of illicit drug use is brought to the emergency department with a severe, sudden-onset resting tremor, "cogwheel" rigidity, and marked bradykinesia. Toxicological analysis reveals he injected a synthetic opioid contaminated with MPTP. This neurotoxin directly causes the destruction of which of the following brain structures?
questions.educationalObjective: MPTP is a neurotoxin that destroys the substantia nigra, leading to an acute onset of drug-induced Parkinson disease.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: MPTP is a toxic contaminant found in certain illicit drugs that is metabolized in the brain to MPP+. This active metabolite selectively destroys dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, leading to acute, irreversible, and severe parkinsonian symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Caudate nucleus: Degeneration here is seen in Huntington disease.
(B, C) Globus pallidus / Subthalamic nucleus: While part of the basal ganglia motor circuit, they are not the specific targets of MPTP-induced destruction.

metadata.reviewPearl: MPTP is a neurotoxin that destroys the substantia nigra, leading to an acute onset of drug-induced Parkinson disease.

questions.reviewPearl: Lewy body dementia is identified by fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, REM sleep behavior disorder, and parkinsonism.
questions.stem: A 70-year-old male is evaluated for cognitive impairment. His family reports that his alertness and cognition fluctuate wildly from day to day. He frequently reports seeing "little children playing in the living room" when no one is there. Additionally, his wife notes that he forcefully thrashes around in bed while asleep. On physical exam, he has a mild resting tremor and rigidity. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Lewy body dementia is identified by fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, REM sleep behavior disorder, and parkinsonism.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Lewy body dementia is characterized by the triad of fluctuating cognition/alertness, prominent visual hallucinations, and the subsequent development of parkinsonian features. It is also highly associated with REM sleep behavior disorder (acting out dreams/thrashing in sleep).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Alzheimer disease: Does not classically present with early, prominent visual hallucinations or fluctuating alertness.
(B) Delirium tremens: An acute alcohol withdrawal syndrome, not a chronic neurodegenerative dementia.

(D) Parkinson disease dementia: Diagnosed when parkinsonian motor symptoms precede the onset of dementia by at least one year; in Lewy body dementia, cognitive and hallucinatory symptoms typically begin concurrently with or before motor symptoms.

metadata.reviewPearl: Lewy body dementia is identified by fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, REM sleep behavior disorder, and parkinsonism.

questions.reviewPearl: Decreased dopamine in the mesocortical pathway correlates with the negative symptoms of schizophrenia.
questions.stem: A 27-year-old man with schizophrenia is evaluated in the clinic. He exhibits significant "negative" symptoms, including anergia, apathy, affective flattening, and a profound lack of spontaneity. These specific symptoms are primarily theorized to be the result of decreased dopaminergic activity in which of the following pathways?
questions.educationalObjective: Decreased dopamine in the mesocortical pathway correlates with the negative symptoms of schizophrenia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The negative symptoms of schizophrenia (apathy, anergia, flat affect) are attributed to decreased dopaminergic activity (or blockage) in the mesocortical pathway.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Mesolimbic pathway: Overactivity here is responsible for the positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions).
(C) Nigrostriatal pathway: Blockade here causes extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS) like parkinsonism.
(D) Tuberoinfundibular pathway: Blockade here causes hyperprolactinemia (galactorrhea, gynecomastia).

metadata.reviewPearl: Decreased dopamine in the mesocortical pathway correlates with the negative symptoms of schizophrenia.

questions.reviewPearl: Alpha waves on an EEG characterize a state of relaxed wakefulness with closed eyes.
questions.stem: A 22-year-old female medical student volunteers for a sleep study. She is instructed to lay comfortably in a quiet, dark room and to close her eyes while relaxing, but to remain awake. Which of the following EEG waveforms is most likely to be dominant at this exact moment?
questions.educationalObjective: Alpha waves on an EEG characterize a state of relaxed wakefulness with closed eyes.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: When a person is awake, relaxed, and has their eyes closed, the dominant EEG pattern consists of Alpha waves.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Beta waves: These have the highest frequency and lowest amplitude and are seen when a person is awake, alert, and has their eyes open (or during REM sleep).
(C) Delta waves: These are seen during deep, slow-wave sleep (Stage N3).
(D) Theta waves: These indicate light sleep (Stage N1).

metadata.reviewPearl: Alpha waves on an EEG characterize a state of relaxed wakefulness with closed eyes.

questions.reviewPearl: Stage N3 sleep is the deepest stage of non-REM sleep and is the phase during which sleepwalking, night terrors, and bedwetting occur.
questions.stem: During an overnight polysomnogram, a patient is observed to be in the deepest stage of non-REM sleep. The EEG shows low-frequency, high-amplitude waves. Which of the following parasomnias is most likely to occur during this specific stage of sleep?
questions.educationalObjective: Stage N3 sleep is the deepest stage of non-REM sleep and is the phase during which sleepwalking, night terrors, and bedwetting occur.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is in Stage N3 sleep (deep, slow-wave sleep), characterized by Delta waves. Parasomnias that classically occur during N3 sleep include sleepwalking (somnambulism), night terrors, and bedwetting (nocturnal enuresis).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Bruxism: Teeth grinding typically occurs during Stage N2 sleep.
(B) Narcolepsy: Characterized by a sudden transition directly into REM sleep.
(C) REM sleep behavior disorder: Occurs during REM sleep, not non-REM N3.

metadata.reviewPearl: Stage N3 sleep is the deepest stage of non-REM sleep and is the phase during which sleepwalking, night terrors, and bedwetting occur.

questions.reviewPearl: REM sleep is facilitated by increased levels of acetylcholine and is associated with loss of motor tone and high brain metabolic activity.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old man undergoes a polysomnography evaluation. The tracing indicates that he is currently in a stage of sleep characterized by increased brain oxygen use, variable pulse and blood pressure, and a complete loss of motor tone. Which of the following neurotransmitters is found at its highest levels during this specific sleep stage?
questions.educationalObjective: REM sleep is facilitated by increased levels of acetylcholine and is associated with loss of motor tone and high brain metabolic activity.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is in REM sleep, indicated by high brain activity, variable vitals, and profound muscle atonia. REM sleep is driven and increased by the neurotransmitter acetylcholine. Conversely, it is decreased by norepinephrine.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Dopamine: Primarily involved in wakefulness and reward pathways, not the induction of REM sleep.
(C) GABA: A major inhibitory neurotransmitter that promotes general sleep and sedation, but acetylcholine specifically drives the REM phase.
(D) Norepinephrine: High levels of norepinephrine actually decrease REM sleep.

metadata.reviewPearl: REM sleep is facilitated by increased levels of acetylcholine and is associated with loss of motor tone and high brain metabolic activity.

questions.reviewPearl: Depression is associated with decreased REM latency, increased total REM sleep, decreased N3 sleep, and terminal insomnia.
questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with untreated Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) is referred for a sleep study to evaluate chronic fatigue. Compared to a healthy individual of the same age, which of the following changes to her sleep architecture is most likely to be observed?

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Prolonged latency to sleep onset without early awakenings

questions.educationalObjective: Depression is associated with decreased REM latency, increased total REM sleep, decreased N3 sleep, and terminal insomnia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Patients with depression exhibit characteristic changes in sleep architecture, most notably a decreased REM latency (they enter REM sleep sooner after falling asleep) and an increased total duration of REM sleep. They also experience decreased slow-wave (N3) sleep and frequent early morning awakenings (terminal insomnia).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Decreased overall REM: Depression is associated with increased REM sleep.
(C) Increased slow-wave sleep: Both depression and advancing age are associated with decreased N3 (slow-wave) sleep.
(D) Prolonged latency... without early awakenings: While sleep latency may be increased, early morning awakenings are a hallmark of depression.

metadata.reviewPearl: Depression is associated with decreased REM latency, increased total REM sleep, decreased N3 sleep, and terminal insomnia.

questions.reviewPearl: Healthy aging is characterized by a decrease in both REM sleep and deep N3 slow-wave sleep, along with increased awakenings.
questions.stem: A 70-year-old man visits his primary care physician complaining that he does not sleep as well as he did in his thirties. He reports waking up several times during the night and waking up earlier in the morning than he would like. He denies any symptoms of depression or anxiety. Which of the following changes in sleep architecture is a normal physiological result of aging in this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: Healthy aging is characterized by a decrease in both REM sleep and deep N3 slow-wave sleep, along with increased awakenings.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Normal aging is associated with distinct changes in sleep architecture, including decreased slow-wave (N3) sleep and decreased REM sleep. Elderly patients also experience increased sleep latency (taking longer to fall asleep) and more frequent nighttime and early morning awakenings.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Increased REM sleep: This is seen in depression, whereas elderly patients experience decreased REM sleep.
(C, D) Increased total sleep time / Shorter sleep latency: Elderly patients typically sleep less overall and take longer to fall asleep.

metadata.reviewPearl: Healthy aging is characterized by a decrease in both REM sleep and deep N3 slow-wave sleep, along with increased awakenings.

questions.reviewPearl: Desmopressin is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for nocturnal enuresis in children who fail behavioral and alarm therapies.
questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician because he wets the bed 3-4 times a week. The parents have tried fluid restriction in the evenings and a bedwetting alarm for the past 6 months without any success. They are requesting medication to help manage the condition before he goes to a sleepaway camp. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Desmopressin is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for nocturnal enuresis in children who fail behavioral and alarm therapies.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: For a child ≥ 7 years old with primary nocturnal enuresis who has failed behavioral modifications and enuresis alarms, pharmacotherapy is indicated. The first-line medication is desmopressin (an ADH analog). Imipramine (a TCA) is considered a second-line agent due to its severe side-effect profile, particularly cardiotoxicity.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Diazepam: Benzodiazepines are not indicated for enuresis; they are used for sleep terrors or sleepwalking (N3 parasomnias).
(C) Imipramine: An effective but second-line option for enuresis due to the risk of lethal overdose and cardiotoxicity.
(D) Oxybutynin: An anticholinergic used for daytime overactive bladder, not first-line for primary nocturnal enuresis.

metadata.reviewPearl: Desmopressin is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for nocturnal enuresis in children who fail behavioral and alarm therapies.

questions.reviewPearl: Positive reinforcement increases the likelihood of a behavior by presenting a rewarding stimulus immediately afterward.
questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman is attempting to train her new puppy. Every time the puppy sits on command, she immediately gives him a small treat. Over time, the puppy learns to sit reliably whenever the command is given. This type of learning, where a behavior is increased by the addition of a desired stimulus, is an example of which of the following?
questions.educationalObjective: Positive reinforcement increases the likelihood of a behavior by presenting a rewarding stimulus immediately afterward.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Positive reinforcement is an operant conditioning technique that involves the addition of a stimulus (a dog treat) to increase the frequency of a target behavior (sitting).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Classical conditioning: Involves pairing an unconditioned stimulus (food) with a neutral stimulus (bell) to elicit an involuntary response (salivation), not modifying voluntary behavior through reward.
(B) Negative reinforcement: Involves the removal of an aversive stimulus to increase a behavior (e.g., turning off an annoying alarm when the seatbelt is buckled).
(C) Positive punishment: Involves the addition of an aversive stimulus to decrease a behavior (e.g., spraying a cat with water to stop it from scratching furniture).

metadata.reviewPearl: Positive reinforcement increases the likelihood of a behavior by presenting a rewarding stimulus immediately afterward.

questions.reviewPearl: Transference is the unconscious redirection of a patient's feelings from a personal relationship toward their physician.
questions.stem: A 45-year-old physician is conducting a therapy session with a 20-year-old female patient. The patient has a history of severe paternal neglect. During the session, the patient becomes visibly angry and shouts, "You never listen to me, you're always cutting me off just like he did!" The patient is projecting feelings meant for her father onto the physician. What is the psychological term for this phenomenon?
questions.educationalObjective: Transference is the unconscious redirection of a patient's feelings from a personal relationship toward their physician.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Transference occurs when a patient projects feelings, expectations, or desires regarding a formative figure in their life (like a parent) onto the physician or therapist.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Countertransference: This is the reversewhen the physician projects their own unresolved feelings onto the patient (e.g., a doctor treating a patient who reminds them of their sibling).
(B) Displacement: Redirection of an impulse onto a neutral third party out of anger or frustration, not necessarily projecting a formative relationship dynamic.
(C) Identification: Unconscious modeling of one's own behavior on another person.

metadata.reviewPearl: Transference is the unconscious redirection of a patient's feelings from a personal relationship toward their physician.

questions.reviewPearl: Intellectualization involves focusing on the intellectual or technical aspects of a distressing situation to avoid experiencing the associated painful emotions.
questions.stem: A 35-year-old female is informed by her oncologist that her breast cancer has metastasized and is now terminal. The patient nods calmly, pulls out a notebook, and says, "Okay, let's review the exact statistical survival rates for the next five years and the biochemical mechanisms of the failure of my current chemotherapy." She shows no visible emotional distress. Which of the following defense mechanisms is she primarily utilizing?
questions.educationalObjective: Intellectualization involves focusing on the intellectual or technical aspects of a distressing situation to avoid experiencing the associated painful emotions.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Intellectualization is an immature defense mechanism where a patient uses facts, logic, and technical details to emotionally distance themselves from a highly stressful or traumatic situation (e.g., focusing on statistics and biochemistry rather than confronting the emotional reality of terminal illness).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Denial: Involves completely refusing to acknowledge the reality of the situation (e.g., "I don't have cancer, the lab made a mistake").

(C) Isolation of affect: Separating feelings from ideas; the patient would describe the event factually without emotion, but intellectualization specifically involves deep-diving into the technical facts and logic.
(D) Rationalization: Creating logical excuses or justifications for unacceptable actions or outcomes.

metadata.reviewPearl: Intellectualization involves focusing on the intellectual or technical aspects of a distressing situation to avoid experiencing the associated painful emotions.

questions.reviewPearl: In the setting of an SSRI overdose, management begins with assessing for the presence of co-ingestants, as isolated SSRI ingestion is rarely life-threatening.
questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after intentionally ingesting 30 tablets of fluoxetine. On initial evaluation, she is alert and oriented with a blood pressure of 118/72 mmHg and a heart rate of 76/min. Physical examination is entirely unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: In the setting of an SSRI overdose, management begins with assessing for the presence of co-ingestants, as isolated SSRI ingestion is rarely life-threatening.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Isolated overdoses of SSRIs are generally well-tolerated and often present with minimal symptoms. However, if a patient presents with altered mental status or abnormal physical findings (not seen here, but a common clinical "pivot"), or even in stable patients with a clear suicidal intent, the most critical next step is to evaluate for co-ingestants such as salicylates, acetaminophen, or ethanol. These substances are more likely to cause significant morbidity and require specific interventions.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Activated charcoal: While sometimes used, it is generally reserved for patients presenting within 12 hours of ingestion and is not the primary diagnostic priority.
(B) Sodium bicarbonate: This is the treatment for TCA overdose (to overcome sodium channel blockade), not SSRI overdose.
(D) Gastric lavage: Rarely performed in modern toxicology due to the risk of aspiration and lack of proven benefit for SSRIs.

metadata.reviewPearl: In the setting of an SSRI overdose, management begins with assessing for the presence of co-ingestants, as isolated SSRI ingestion is rarely life-threatening.

questions.reviewPearl: Atypical antipsychotics are associated with increased all-cause mortality and an increased risk of pneumonia in the geriatric population.
questions.stem: An 82-year-old male with severe dementia and behavioral disturbances is started on risperidone to manage his agitation. Two months later, he is hospitalized for a productive cough, high fever, and shortness of breath. Chest X-ray reveals a lobar consolidation. Which of the following is the most likely reason for his increased risk of this condition?
questions.educationalObjective: Atypical antipsychotics are associated with increased all-cause mortality and an increased risk of pneumonia in the geriatric population.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The use of atypical antipsychotics in the elderly is associated with a significantly increased risk of community-acquired pneumonia. While the exact mechanism is not fully established, it is theorized to be due to the anticholinergic and antihistamine effects of these medications, which can impair swallowing (dysphagia) and suppress the cough reflex, thereby increasing the risk of aspiration.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Agranulocytosis: This is a specific risk associated with clozapine, not risperidone.
(C) Pulmonary toxicity: Antipsychotics are not known to be direct pulmonary toxins.
(D) Cytokine release: This describes a different pathophysiological process (like NMS or ARDS) rather than the mechanism for pneumonia in this setting.

metadata.reviewPearl: Atypical antipsychotics are associated with increased all-cause mortality and an increased risk of pneumonia in the geriatric population.

questions.reviewPearl: Abrupt cessation of short-half-life antidepressants (paroxetine, venlafaxine) leads to a cluster of somatic and psychological symptoms known as discontinuation syndrome.
questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with a history of Major Depressive Disorder presents with "flu-like" symptoms, including nausea, fatigue, and muscle aches. She also reports feeling "jittery" and having vivid dreams. She mentions that she lost her insurance a week ago and ran out of her Paroxetine. Which of the following best describes this patient's presentation?
questions.educationalObjective: Abrupt cessation of short-half-life antidepressants (paroxetine, venlafaxine) leads to a cluster of somatic and psychological symptoms known as discontinuation syndrome.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: The patient is experiencing antidepressant discontinuation syndrome, which is most common with medications that have a short half-life (e.g., paroxetine or venlafaxine). The symptoms can be remembered by the mnemonic FINISH: Flu-like symptoms, Insomnia, Nausea, Instability (dizziness), Sensory disturbances (parasthesias), and Hyperarousal (anxiety/agitation).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (B) Recurrent MDD: While depression can return, the acute onset of flu-like and sensory symptoms immediately after stopping medication is diagnostic of discontinuation syndrome.
(C) Serotonin syndrome: Requires the addition of serotonergic agents and presents with hyperreflexia and autonomic instability, not withdrawal symptoms.
(D) Somatic symptom disorder: Involves chronic (>6 months) preoccupation with physical symptoms, not an acute withdrawal state.

metadata.reviewPearl: Abrupt cessation of short-half-life antidepressants (paroxetine, venlafaxine) leads to a cluster of somatic and psychological symptoms known as discontinuation syndrome.

questions.reviewPearl: Common precipitants of lithium toxicity include NSAIDs (except aspirin), thiazide diuretics, and ACE inhibitors.
questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with Bipolar Disorder has been stable on Lithium for several years. He recently visited an urgent care clinic for a minor ankle sprain and was told to take an over-the-counter medication for the pain. One week later, he presents with a coarse tremor, ataxia, and confusion. Which of the following medications most likely caused this interaction?
questions.educationalObjective: Common precipitants of lithium toxicity include NSAIDs (except aspirin), thiazide diuretics, and ACE inhibitors.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer Explanation
body: Lithium is exclusively renally cleared. Its levels are increased by medications that decrease renal blood flow or increase proximal tubule reabsorption. NSAIDs (like ibuprofen or naproxen) decrease prostaglandins, leading to afferent arteriolar vasoconstriction and a decrease in GFR, which increases lithium levels.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers Explanation
body: (A) Acetaminophen: Does not affect renal prostaglandin synthesis and is safe to use with lithium.
(B) Aspirin: Unique among NSAIDs in that it does not significantly affect lithium levels.
(D) Tramadol: While it has serotonergic properties, it does not typically increase lithium levels.

metadata.reviewPearl: Common precipitants of lithium toxicity include NSAIDs (except aspirin), thiazide diuretics, and ACE inhibitors.

questions.reviewPearl: In TCA overdose, sodium bicarbonate is used to treat QRS prolongation and prevent life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias.
questions.stem: A 26-year-old male is brought to the emergency department after a suspected suicide attempt. He is currently obtunded.
